subject: Dermatology
front: What are the three main layers of the skin?
back: Epidermis, dermis, and hypodermis.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the layers of the epidermis from superficial to deep.
back: Stratum corneum, stratum lucidum, stratum granulosum, stratum spinosum, and stratum basale.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of cells make up the stratum corneum, and is it a true cellular layer?
back: Flat, fully keratinized, anucleate cells; it is not considered a true cellular layer.

subject: Dermatology
front: Where is the stratum lucidum found and what is its characteristic?
back: It is translucent and found only in the palms and soles.

subject: Dermatology
front: What two types of granules are present in the stratum granulosum?
back: Keratohyaline granules and lamellar granules.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the keratohyaline granule contain, and what protein is it converted into?
back: It contains profilaggrin, which is converted into filaggrin (a filament-aggregating protein).

subject: Dermatology
front: Filaggrin abnormality is implicated in which two skin conditions?
back: Ichthyosis vulgaris and atopic dermatitis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the function of the lamellar granules of the stratum granulosum?
back: They produce lipid, which is involved in the skin's barrier function.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is characteristic of the stratum spinosum on microscopy, and what structure produces this appearance?
back: Prominent 'spines,' produced by desmosomes (intraepidermal proteins).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the cell arrangement in the stratum basale.
back: A single layer of columnar cells with a central nucleus.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is another name for the stratum basale?
back: Stratum germinativum.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which two epidermal layers together make up the 'stratum malpighii'?
back: Stratum spinosum and stratum basale.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the normal epidermal transit time from stratum basale to stratum corneum?
back: 28 days.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the epidermal transit time in psoriasis?
back: 3-5 days.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are desmosomes and what is their function?
back: Intraepidermal, intercellular connections between keratinocytes that provide adhesion.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which group of blistering disorders involves antibodies against desmosomes?
back: The pemphigus group (anti-desmosome antibodies).

subject: Dermatology
front: What percentage of epidermal cells are keratinocytes, and what is their embryologic origin?
back: 90%; they are ectodermal cells.

subject: Dermatology
front: What structural component of keratinocytes provides them structural support?
back: Keratin intermediate filaments.

subject: Dermatology
front: Name the three types of non-keratinocyte cells found in the epidermis.
back: Langerhans cells, melanocytes, and Merkel cells.

subject: Dermatology
front: Where are Langerhans cells located in the epidermis?
back: Stratum spinosum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the embryologic origin of Langerhans cells?
back: Bone marrow (mesoderm).

subject: Dermatology
front: What granules do Langerhans cells contain, and what is their function?
back: They contain Birbeck granules and function as antigen presenting cells (APCs).

subject: Dermatology
front: What immunohistochemical markers are used in Langerhans cell histiocytosis (LCH)?
back: S100, CD1a, and CD207/langerin (most specific).

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the abbreviation 'LCH' stand for?
back: Langerhans cell histiocytosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the electron microscopic appearance of Birbeck granules.
back: A 'tennis racquet' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
front: Where are melanocytes located in the epidermis, and what is their embryologic origin?
back: Stratum basale; derived from neural crest.

subject: Dermatology
front: What do melanocytes contain, and what do they produce?
back: They contain melanosomes and produce melanin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What immunohistochemical markers are used to identify melanoma?
back: S100, HMB-45, and Melan-A.

subject: Dermatology
front: Where are Merkel cells located in the epidermis, and what is their embryologic origin?
back: Stratum basale; derived from ectoderm.

subject: Dermatology
front: What granules do Merkel cells contain, and what is their function?
back: Neurosecretory granules; they act as slow-adapting, low-threshold mechanoreceptors for touch.

subject: Dermatology
front: What immunohistochemical marker is used in Merkel cell carcinoma?
back: Cytokeratin-20.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the function of the dermoepidermal junction/basement membrane zone?
back: A specialized zone that connects the stratum basale to the underlying dermis.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the four zones of the basement membrane zone from epidermis to dermis.
back: Hemidesmosome-keratin intermediate filament complex (HD-KIF complex), lamina lucida, lamina densa, and sublamina densa.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two layers of the dermis?
back: Papillary dermis and reticular dermis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare collagen fiber thickness between the papillary and reticular dermis.
back: Papillary dermis has finer collagen fibers; reticular dermis has thicker collagen fibers.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the arrangement of collagen fibers in the papillary vs reticular dermis.
back: Papillary dermis: loose and regular arrangement; reticular dermis: dense and irregular arrangement.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two compartments of the hypodermis/subcutaneous tissue, and what does each contain?
back: Septal compartment (contains collagen fibers) and lobular compartment (contains adipocytes).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is panniculitis?
back: Inflammation of the subcutaneous fat.

subject: Dermatology
front: How are skin stem cells defined?
back: Undifferentiated, self-renewing, multipotent cells residing in specific areas of the skin.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the three locations where skin stem cells reside.
back: Stratum basale (interfollicular epidermis), the hair follicle bulge area (at the arrector pili muscle attachment), and the base of the sebaceous gland.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the three functions of skin stem cells.
back: Maintenance of epidermal homeostasis, wound repair, and regeneration of hair follicles and sebaceous glands.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the embryologic derivative of the epidermis?
back: Ectoderm.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the embryologic derivative of the dermis, and what is the exception?
back: Mesoderm; the exception is the face and frontal scalp, which derive from neural crest.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the embryologic derivative of the hypodermis?
back: Mesoderm.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is hyperkeratosis?
back: Thickening of the stratum corneum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is parakeratosis?
back: A nucleated stratum corneum (retention of nuclei in the stratum corneum).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is acanthosis?
back: Thickening of the stratum spinosum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is spongiosis, and give an example of a condition where it is seen.
back: Intraepidermal, intercellular edema; seen in acute eczema.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is acantholysis?
back: Loss of attachment between keratinocytes.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the three categories of causes of acantholysis with an example for each.
back: Autoimmune (pemphigus group), infection (bullous impetigo, staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome/SSSS, HSV), and genetic (Hailey-Hailey disease, Darier's disease).

subject: Dermatology
front: In acantholysis, what structure is targeted and what type of keratinocytes result?
back: Desmosomes are targeted, resulting in polyhedral keratinocytes.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are acantholytic (Tzanck) cells and what do they look like?
back: Cells resulting from acantholysis; they are circular with a prominent nucleus and a perinuclear halo.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of scale is characteristically seen in pityriasis versicolor?
back: Branny scale.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of scale is characteristically seen in pityriasis lichenoides chronica (PLC)?
back: Micaceous scale.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of scale is characteristically seen in psoriasis?
back: Silvery-white scale.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of scale is characteristically seen in pityriasis rosea?
back: Collarette scale.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the shape of an annular lesion and where its active region is located.
back: Ring-shaped, with the active region at the periphery.

subject: Dermatology
front: Give three causes of annular skin lesions.
back: Pityriasis rosea, tinea corporis, and mid-borderline Hansen's disease (BB-HD leprosy).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the shape of a nummular lesion and where its active region is located.
back: Disc/coin-shaped, with active regions at both the centre and periphery.

subject: Dermatology
front: Give two causes of nummular skin lesions.
back: Discoid eczema and discoid lupus erythematosus.

subject: Dermatology
front: What do Langer's lines represent?
back: Lines of skin tension, representing the orientation of collagen fibers in the dermis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the pattern of Langer's lines over the abdomen and lower back vs the orifices.
back: Transverse over the abdomen and lower back; circular around orifices.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the clinical/surgical application of Langer's lines?
back: Surgical incisions are made along them to help prevent hypertrophic scars.

subject: Dermatology
front: What do Blaschko's lines represent?
back: The pathway of epidermal cell migration during embryological development.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the pattern of Blaschko's lines at the upper back, abdomen, limbs, and scalp.
back: V-shaped on the upper back, S-shaped on the abdomen, longitudinal on the limbs, and spiral on the scalp.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which condition shows skin lesions distributed along Blaschko's lines, and what is its inheritance pattern?
back: Incontinentia pigmenti; it is X-linked dominant.

subject: Dermatology
front: What clinical technique is used to differentiate erythema from purpura?
back: Diascopy.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is diascopy performed?
back: A glass slide is pressed over the skin lesion.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the diascopy finding in erythema?
back: A blanching response.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the diascopy finding in purpura, and why does this occur?
back: A non-blanching response, due to RBC extravasation.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are apple jelly nodules and what causes their color?
back: Nodules identified on diascopy that appear yellow-brown due to underlying dermal granulomas.

subject: Dermatology
front: Name three conditions in which apple jelly nodules are seen.
back: Lupus vulgaris (cutaneous TB), sarcoidosis, and cutaneous leishmaniasis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is Wood's lamp and at what wavelength does it operate?
back: A non-invasive diagnostic tool for examining skin under UV light, operating at a wavelength of 364 nm.

subject: Dermatology
front: What filter material is used in Wood's lamp?
back: Barium silicate plus 9% nickel oxide.

subject: Dermatology
front: What color fluorescence does erythrasma show under Wood's lamp?
back: Coral red fluorescence.

subject: Dermatology
front: What color fluorescence does pityriasis versicolor show under Wood's lamp?
back: Yellow fluorescence.

subject: Dermatology
front: What color fluorescence does tinea capitis caused by Microsporum species show under Wood's lamp?
back: Blue-green fluorescence.

subject: Dermatology
front: What color fluorescence does tinea capitis caused by Trichophyton schoenleinii show under Wood's lamp?
back: Dull blue fluorescence.

subject: Dermatology
front: What color does vitiligo show under Wood's lamp?
back: Milky white.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the mechanism of action of cryotherapy?
back: Rapid cooling of the target tissue, causing cellular destruction.

subject: Dermatology
front: What agent and temperature is used in cryotherapy?
back: Liquid nitrogen at -196°C.

subject: Dermatology
front: Give a common indication for cryotherapy.
back: Warts.

subject: Dermatology
front: What agent is used in phototherapy?
back: Ultraviolet radiation.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the wavelength range used in narrow band UVB phototherapy?

subject: Dermatology
front: What does PUVA phototherapy consist of?
back: Psoralen (a photosensitizer) followed by UV-A radiation (320-400 nm).

subject: Dermatology
front: List three indications for phototherapy.
back: Psoriasis, vitiligo, and atopic dermatitis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are skin appendages and what structures do they include?
back: Specific structures derived from the epidermis, including hair, nails, sweat glands, and sebaceous glands.

subject: Dermatology
front: How does the eccrine sweat gland open onto the skin?
back: It opens directly onto the skin surface.

subject: Dermatology
front: How does the apocrine gland open?
back: It opens into the upper portion of the hair follicle.

subject: Dermatology
front: What muscle attaches at the hair follicle as part of skin appendage anatomy?
back: The erector (arrector) pili muscle.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the four classifications of non-cicatricial (non-scarring) alopecia?
back: Patchy, diffuse, patterned, and due to systemic disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: Give examples of patchy non-scarring alopecia.
back: Alopecia areata, trichotillomania, tinea capitis (non-inflammatory type), and secondary syphilis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Give examples of diffuse non-scarring alopecia.
back: Anagen effluvium and telogen effluvium.

subject: Dermatology
front: Give an example of patterned non-scarring alopecia.
back: Androgenetic alopecia.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the underlying pathology of alopecia areata?
back: An autoimmune condition targeting the anagen hair bulb.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical presentation of alopecia areata.
back: Patchy alopecia with circular patches of complete hair loss.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is an 'exclamation mark hair' and in which condition is it seen?
back: A hair with a broad distal end and a tapering proximal end, seen in alopecia areata.

subject: Dermatology
front: What nail finding can be seen in alopecia areata?
back: Fine, superficial, regular pitting.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for localized alopecia areata?
back: Intralesional steroids (triamcinolone).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for extensive alopecia areata?
back: Systemic steroids.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is trichotillomania?
back: Recurrent hair pulling, associated with obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the alopecia pattern in trichotillomania.
back: Patchy alopecia with bizarre-shaped lesions and incomplete loss of hair.

subject: Dermatology
front: What hair shaft finding is characteristic of trichotillomania?
back: Hair shafts of varying length.

subject: Dermatology
front: What additional skin finding is seen in trichotillomania?
back: Perifollicular hemorrhages.

subject: Dermatology
front: What complication can occur from trichophagia in patients with trichotillomania?
back: Formation of a trichobezoar (indigested hair mass), which can cause gastric outlet or small intestine obstruction.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which vitamin deficiency is associated with perifollicular hemorrhage?
back: Vitamin C deficiency.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the effect of non-inflammatory tinea capitis on hair (scarring vs non-scarring)?
back: It is non-inflammatory and results in non-scarring alopecia.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features of black dot type tinea capitis.
back: A child with patchy alopecia, itchy scalp, and broken hair at the level of the scalp, with black dots visible.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of hair infection (endothrix/ectothrix) causes black dot tinea capitis, and what organisms are responsible?
back: Endothrix infection (fungus grows into the hair follicle), caused by T. tonsurans and T. violaceum.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features of grey patch type tinea capitis.
back: A child with patchy alopecia, oval plaques, and diffuse, dull-grey, adherent scales.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of hair infection causes grey patch tinea capitis, and which organism is responsible?
back: Ectothrix infection (fungus covers the hair follicle), caused by Microsporum canis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the alopecia pattern seen in secondary syphilis.
back: A moth-eaten pattern of patchy alopecia, with a history of high-risk sexual behaviour.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the cause and timing of anagen effluvium vs telogen effluvium.
back: Anagen effluvium is secondary to chemotherapy/radiotherapy 2-3 weeks prior; telogen effluvium is secondary to infection (e.g., COVID-19, malaria, typhoid) 3-4 months prior.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the pathophysiology of anagen effluvium?
back: An acute insult causes disruption of the mitotic activity of hair follicle matrix cells.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the pathophysiology of telogen effluvium?
back: A severe systemic stressor disrupts the hair follicle cycle, causing premature transition of hair from anagen to telogen phase.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the onset and progression of patterned (androgenetic) alopecia?
back: Insidious (gradual) onset with slow progression.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the pattern of male androgenetic alopecia.
back: Fronto-temporal recession and balding of the vertex.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the pattern of female androgenetic alopecia.
back: Widening of the central parting, with the frontal hairline maintained.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two main drug treatments for androgenetic alopecia?
back: Minoxidil and finasteride.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the mechanism of action of finasteride?
back: It inhibits 5α-reductase (type 2 isoenzyme).

subject: Dermatology
front: List the four categories of cicatricial (scarring) alopecia with an example for each.
back: Papulosquamous (lichen planus), granulomatous (sarcoidosis), connective tissue disease (discoid lupus erythematosus/discoid lesions of SLE, linear morphea also called en coup de sabre), and infection (tinea capitis, inflammatory type).

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two types of inflammatory tinea capitis?
back: Favus and kerion.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes favus, and in which population is it classically described?
back: Trichophyton schoenleinii; classically described in children from Kashmir.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical feature of favus and its name.
back: Yellow cup-shaped crusts on the scalp, called scutula.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the Wood's lamp finding in favus?
back: Dull blue fluorescence.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organisms cause kerion?
back: Zoophilic fungi such as Trichophyton mentagrophyte and Trichophyton verrucosum.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the typical clinical presentation of kerion.
back: A child with a history of exposure to a pet dog, presenting with boggy scalp swelling, easily pluckable hair, and positive regional lymphadenopathy.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is frontal fibrosing alopecia a variant of?
back: Lichen plano-pilaris.

subject: Dermatology
front: In which population is frontal fibrosing alopecia typically seen?
back: Post-menopausal women.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical pattern of frontal fibrosing alopecia and its association.
back: Scarring alopecia involving the fronto-temporal areas, associated with madarosis (loss of eyebrows).

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the secretion mechanism of eccrine vs apocrine glands.
back: Eccrine glands secrete by merocrine secretion (intact cell border); apocrine glands secrete by apocrine secretion (cell apex is pinched off).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the function of eccrine glands, and what does obstruction of these glands cause?
back: They produce sweat; obstruction causes miliaria.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the function of apocrine glands, and what does obstruction of these glands cause?
back: They produce body odour; obstruction causes apocrine miliaria.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is another name for apocrine miliaria?
back: Fox-Fordyce disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the underlying pathology of apocrine miliaria?
back: Obstruction of apocrine ducts.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the lesions and sites of apocrine miliaria.
back: Pruritic, skin-coloured papules, located in the axilla and areola of the nipple.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the function of sebaceous glands and what is their secretion mechanism?
back: They produce sebum via holocrine secretion.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are 'ectopic sebaceous glands' and give a named example.
back: Sebaceous glands not associated with hair follicles; an example is Fordyce spots, which appear as yellow micropapules over the upper lips/buccal mucosa.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is acne vulgaris and what microbe is implicated in its pathogenesis?
back: A chronic inflammatory disease of the pilosebaceous unit, implicating Propionibacterium/Cutibacterium acnes.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of skin lesions characterize acne vulgaris?
back: Polymorphic lesions.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is Grade 1 acne defined, and what is its treatment?
back: Mild comedonal acne; treated with topical retinoids (adapalene/tretinoin) OR benzoyl peroxide (BPO).

subject: Dermatology
front: How is Grade 2 acne defined, and what is its treatment?
back: Mild to moderate papulopustular acne; treated with combination topical therapy of BPO plus topical retinoids/antibiotics (clindamycin), providing antibacterial and comedolytic action.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is Grade 3 acne defined, and what is its treatment?
back: Moderate to severe papulopustular/moderate nodular acne; treated with an oral antibiotic (minocycline/doxycycline/azithromycin) plus a topical retinoid (adapalene) plus BPO.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is Grade 4 acne defined, and what is its treatment?
back: Nodulo-cystic acne; treated with oral retinoids (isotretinoin).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the chemical name of isotretinoin?
back: 13-cis retinoic acid.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the most common side effect of isotretinoin?
back: Cheilitis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What monitoring tests are required during isotretinoin therapy, and why?
back: Fasting lipid profile (to evaluate for increased triglycerides and total cholesterol) and liver function tests (to rule out rare hepatitis).

subject: Dermatology
front: Why is isotretinoin contraindicated in pregnancy?
back: Because it is teratogenic.

subject: Dermatology
front: Why are topical steroids not used in the management of acne?
back: Because they can induce acne.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is SAHA syndrome?
back: An obese patient with acne who is non-responsive to oral retinoids/antibiotics.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the SAHA mnemonic stand for, and what does it represent?
back: Seborrhea, Acne, Hirsutism, Androgenetic alopecia (early onset) — representing cutaneous changes of hyperandrogenism.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is rosacea?
back: A chronic inflammatory facial dermatosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the typical sites affected by rosacea?
back: The central convexities of the face.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the common triggers of rosacea and their shared mechanism.
back: Sunlight, spicy food, alcohol, and hot beverages, which act as vasodilators.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the erythemato-telangiectatic type of rosacea.
back: Flushing plus telangiectasia.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the papulopustular type of rosacea.
back: Multiple papules and pustules.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the phymatous type of rosacea (also called rhinophyma or potato nose) and its underlying pathology.
back: Thickened skin over the nose with irregular surface nodularities, due to sebaceous gland hypertrophy and fibrosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the presence of comedones in acne vs rosacea.
back: Comedones are present in acne but absent in rosacea.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare flushing and telangiectasia in acne vs rosacea.
back: Absent in acne; present in rosacea.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the distribution of acne vs rosacea.
back: Acne affects the face, upper trunk, chest, and shoulders; rosacea affects the convexities of the face (cheek, nose, forehead).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the topical treatment for rosacea?
back: Metronidazole.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the systemic treatment for rosacea?
back: Doxycycline.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are Mees lines and what condition are they associated with?
back: Transverse, white bands on the nail, associated with chronic arsenic poisoning.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are Muehrcke's bands and what condition are they associated with?
back: Transverse, paired white bands, associated with hypoalbuminemia.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the appearance of Lindsay's (half-and-half) nails and their associated condition.
back: Proximal 50% of the nail is white and distal 50% is brown; associated with chronic renal failure.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the appearance of Terry's nails and their associated condition.
back: Proximal 90% of the nail is white and distal 10% is brown; associated with chronic liver disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: From which amino acid is melanin formed?
back: Tyrosine.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is melasma?
back: An acquired hyperpigmentation condition causing symmetric hyperpigmented macules.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the triggers of melasma.
back: Sunlight, thyroid dysfunction, and pregnancy (in which it is called chloasma).

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the typical sites of melasma?
back: The malar area, nose, and mandibular area.

subject: Dermatology
front: What condition should be considered as a differential/mimic of melasma?
back: The 'chik sign' seen in chikungunya.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare melanocyte number and activity in freckles vs lentigines.
back: In freckles, melanocyte number is normal but activity is increased; in lentigines, melanocyte number is increased but activity is normal.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which condition is associated with freckles (per this classification)?
back: Xeroderma pigmentosum.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which syndrome is associated with lentigines, and describe its inheritance and features.
back: Peutz-Jeghers syndrome; autosomal dominant, with mucosal lentigines and hamartomatous GI polyps.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is a congenital melanocytic nevus (CMN)?
back: A congenital, benign melanocytic proliferation with well-defined borders that is hypertrichotic and hyperpigmented.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the malignant transformation risk of a giant CMN (size >20 cm)?
back: 2-5% risk of transformation into melanoma.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is Becker's nevus?
back: An acquired, benign, cutaneous hamartoma presenting as a unilateral hyperpigmented, hypertrichotic lesion.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the typical sites of Becker's nevus?
back: The chest and upper shoulder.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is dermal melanocytosis and what appearance does it produce?
back: Presence of melanocytes abnormally located in the dermis, producing blue/slate-gray lesions, also called ceruloderma.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the features of a Mongolian spot.
back: Also called congenital dermal melanocytosis; affects infants; located in the lumbosacral region; resolves spontaneously.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the features of Nevus of Ota.
back: Unilateral, distributed along cranial nerves V1 and V2, involving the face and (in 2/3rd of patients) the sclera.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the features of Nevus of Ito.
back: Unilateral, distributed over the posterior supraclavicular and lateral brachial cutaneous nerve area, affecting the shoulder/upper arm region.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the congenital causes of hypo/depigmentation.
back: Albinism, piebaldism, nevus depigmentosus, and nevus anemicus.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the acquired causes of hypo/depigmentation.
back: Vitiligo and chemical leukoderma.

subject: Dermatology
front: What enzyme is deficient in albinism?
back: Tyrosinase.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two types of albinism?
back: Ocular albinism and oculo-cutaneous albinism (affecting eyes, skin, and hair).

subject: Dermatology
front: Which syndrome is associated with albinism, and what is its inheritance and gene defect?
back: Chediak-Higashi syndrome, autosomal recessive, caused by mutation of the LYST gene.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the features of Chediak-Higashi syndrome?
back: Oculocutaneous albinism, recurrent infections, and neutrophils with giant granules.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the inheritance pattern and underlying pathology of piebaldism?
back: Autosomal dominant; the pathology is a neural crest defect.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the characteristic lesion of piebaldism.
back: A triangular depigmented area with white hair, called a white forelock.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is nevus depigmentosus (also called nevus achromicus)?
back: A congenital, non-progressive, stable, hypopigmented macule.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the underlying defect in nevus depigmentosus?
back: A functional defect of the melanocyte, leading to defective transfer of melanosomes to keratinocytes.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the lesion characteristics of nevus depigmentosus.
back: Solitary, well-defined, serrated borders that do not cross the midline.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the Wood's lamp finding in nevus depigmentosus?
back: Off-white accentuation.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is nevus anemicus?
back: A congenital vascular anomaly presenting as a white patch of vasoconstriction.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the Wood's lamp finding in nevus anemicus?
back: Negative (no accentuation).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the diascopy finding in nevus anemicus.
back: When a glass slide is placed at the border, the surrounding normal skin blanches, causing loss of the outline of the lesion.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the underlying pathology of vitiligo?
back: An acquired, chronic depigmentary disorder due to absent melanocytes (autoimmune).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the onset and progression of segmental vitiligo.
back: Early childhood onset with rapid progression that later stabilizes.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the laterality and leukotrichia features of segmental vitiligo?
back: It is a unilateral lesion, and leukotrichia may be present or absent.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the typical sites and alternate name for non-segmental vitiligo?
back: Face, head, and extremities; also called acrofacial vitiligo.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is mucosal vitiligo defined?
back: Involvement of more than one mucosal site.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is vitiligo vulgaris defined, and how common is it?
back: It is the most common type of vitiligo, characterized by bilateral symmetrical lesions.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is vitiligo universalis defined?
back: Involvement of more than 80-90% of the body surface.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is focal vitiligo?
back: Small, isolated depigmented lesions, classified under unclassified/undetermined vitiligo.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical appearance of vitiligo lesions.
back: Chalky white depigmented macules.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does a positive true Koebner's phenomenon indicate in vitiligo?
back: A bad prognosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the presence of leukotrichia indicate in vitiligo?
back: A bad prognosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What topical treatments are used for vitiligo?
back: Corticosteroids and tacrolimus (a calcineurin inhibitor).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of phototherapy is used for vitiligo?
back: Narrow band UV-B.

subject: Dermatology
front: What systemic treatments are used for vitiligo?
back: Steroids and azathioprine.

subject: Dermatology
front: What causes chemical leukoderma?
back: Chemical-induced destruction of melanocytes.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which chemical in bindis causes chemical leukoderma?
back: Para-tertiary butylphenol (PTBP).

subject: Dermatology
front: Which chemical in rubber footwear causes chemical leukoderma?
back: Mono-benzyl ether of hydroquinone (MBEH).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of disease is psoriasis, and what are its two areas of involvement?
back: A chronic, T-cell mediated inflammatory disease with skin and systemic involvement.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which HLA type is associated with early onset psoriasis?
back: HLA-Cw6.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which HLA type is associated with psoriatic spondylitis?
back: HLA-B27.

subject: Dermatology
front: What happens to the epidermis in the pathogenesis of psoriasis?
back: Hyperproliferation occurs, decreasing the epidermal transit time to 3-5 days.

subject: Dermatology
front: What happens in the dermis in the pathogenesis of psoriasis?
back: Inflammation occurs.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the most common variant of psoriasis, and describe its lesion.
back: Chronic plaque psoriasis/psoriasis vulgaris; lesion is red, scaly papules and plaques.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the color of scales in chronic plaque psoriasis?
back: Silvery-white.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical distribution of chronic plaque psoriasis?
back: Extensor surfaces (knees, elbows, scalp).

subject: Dermatology
front: What two signs are positive in chronic plaque psoriasis?
back: Auspitz sign and Koebner's phenomenon.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is the Auspitz sign elicited (the grattage test)?
back: A glass slide is used to scrape the lesion.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the three findings of the Auspitz sign/grattage test in order of occurrence.
back: 1) Silvery white scales, 2) Bulkeley's membrane, 3) pinpoint bleeding spots.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is Koebner's phenomenon (isomorphic response)?
back: Development of new lesions along the lines of trauma.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the mechanism of true Koebner's phenomenon vs pseudo-Koebner's phenomenon.
back: True Koebner's is immunological in mechanism; pseudo-Koebner's occurs via auto-inoculation.

subject: Dermatology
front: List three causes of true Koebner's phenomenon.
back: Psoriasis, lichen planus, and vitiligo.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the viral causes of pseudo-Koebner's phenomenon.
back: Molluscum contagiosum and viral warts.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are two rare causes of Koebner's phenomenon?
back: Kaposi sarcoma and lichen nitidus.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the most common nail change seen in psoriasis?
back: Coarse, irregular, deep pitting.

subject: Dermatology
front: What causes subungual hyperkeratosis in psoriatic nail disease?
back: Increased keratin accumulation below the nail plate.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does subungual hyperkeratosis lead to in psoriatic nails?
back: Distal onycholysis (separation from the nail bed).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the oil drop sign/salmon patch in psoriatic nail disease, and what is its significance?
back: A nail finding that is pathognomonic of psoriasis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the presentation of guttate psoriasis.
back: Occurs in a child with a history of pharyngitis (Group A beta-hemolytic streptococcus), presenting with rain-drop lesions on the trunk.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the presentation of pustular psoriasis.
back: A patient with psoriasis develops sudden-onset pustules and fever.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the classic trigger for pustular psoriasis?
back: Sudden stoppage of systemic steroids.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the progression of lesions in pustular psoriasis.
back: Pustules coalesce to form lakes/sheets of pus.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the acute generalized form of pustular psoriasis called?
back: Von Zumbusch type.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the pregnancy form of pustular psoriasis called?
back: Impetigo herpetiformis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the presentation of erythroderma/exfoliative dermatitis in a psoriasis patient.
back: A patient with psoriasis develops erythema with or without scaling involving ≥90% of body surface area (BSA).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of arthritis is psoriatic arthritis, and what is the classical joint involved?
back: Seronegative arthritis (RA factor negative); the classical joint is the DIP joint.

subject: Dermatology
front: What additional finding is present in psoriatic arthritis?
back: Enthesitis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does hyperkeratosis represent histologically in psoriasis?
back: Thickening of the stratum corneum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does parakeratosis represent histologically in psoriasis?
back: Persistence of nuclei in the stratum corneum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the spongiform pustule of Kogoj, and where is it located?
back: A neutrophilic collection in the stratum spinosum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is Munro's microabscess, and where is it located?
back: A neutrophilic collection in the stratum corneum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the state of the stratum granulosum in psoriasis histopathology?
back: Absent or decreased.

subject: Dermatology
front: What histological feature of the stratum spinosum is seen in psoriasis?
back: Acanthosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What happens to the rete ridges in psoriasis histopathology?
back: Regular elongation of rete ridges.

subject: Dermatology
front: What causes the Auspitz sign histologically in psoriasis?
back: Multiple dilated blood vessels of the dermal papilla.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment threshold for using topical therapy alone in psoriasis?
back: Body surface area involvement of less than 10%.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment threshold and modalities for phototherapy in psoriasis?
back: Body surface area involvement greater than 10%; modalities are NB-UVB and PUVA.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two main systemic drugs used in psoriasis treatment?
back: Methotrexate and cyclosporine.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the mechanism of action of methotrexate (MTX)?
back: Inhibition of dihydrofolate reductase (DHFR).

subject: Dermatology
front: List the adverse effects of methotrexate.
back: Bone marrow suppression/pancytopenia, teratogenicity, and hepatotoxicity.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the indications for methotrexate in psoriasis.
back: Plaque psoriasis, erythrodermic psoriasis, and psoriatic arthritis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the mechanism of action of cyclosporine, and what is notable about its onset of action?
back: Calcineurin inhibition; it has a rapid action.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the adverse effects of cyclosporine.
back: Nephrotoxicity and hypertension.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the primary indication for cyclosporine in psoriasis?
back: Severe erythrodermic psoriasis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the first and second line drug of choice for pustular psoriasis (non-pregnant)?
back: 1st DOC: acitretin (a retinoid); 2nd DOC: methotrexate.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the first and second line drug of choice for pustular psoriasis of pregnancy?
back: 1st DOC: systemic steroids; 2nd DOC: cyclosporine.

subject: Dermatology
front: Why are acitretin and methotrexate contraindicated in pregnancy?
back: Because they are teratogenic.

subject: Dermatology
front: Are systemic steroids contraindicated in psoriasis, and what is the exception?
back: Yes, they are contraindicated in psoriasis except in pustular psoriasis of pregnancy.

subject: Dermatology
front: By what route are biologic drugs for psoriasis administered?
back: Parenteral.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which biologic targets IL-12/23?
back: Ustekinumab.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which biologic targets IL-23?
back: Guselkumab.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which biologics target Anti-TNF-α?
back: Infliximab, etanercept, and adalimumab.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which biologics target IL-17A?
back: Ixekizumab and secukinumab.

subject: Dermatology
front: By what route are small molecule drugs for psoriasis administered?
back: Oral.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which small molecule is a PDE-4 inhibitor used in psoriasis?
back: Apremilast.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which small molecule is a JAK-STAT 1/3 inhibitor, and what is its specific indication?
back: Tofacitinib; indicated for psoriatic arthritis.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the side effects of PUVA therapy.
back: Photoaging, lentigines, cataract, and cutaneous malignancies (SCC, BCC).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of disease is lichen planus and what structures does it involve?
back: A chronic, immune-mediated, inflammatory disease of the skin, hair, mucosa, and nails.

subject: Dermatology
front: List two known triggers of lichen planus.
back: Dental amalgam (mercury) and hepatitis C virus (HCV).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is drug-induced lichen planus also called?
back: Lichenoid drug eruption.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which anti-hypertensive drug classes can cause drug-induced lichen planus?
back: ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, and alpha-methyldopa.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which anti-arrhythmic drug can cause drug-induced lichen planus?
back: Quinidine.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which diuretic class can cause drug-induced lichen planus?
back: Thiazides.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which anti-malarial drugs can cause drug-induced lichen planus?
back: Chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which metal can cause drug-induced lichen planus?
back: Gold salts.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the 6P mnemonic for the skin lesions of lichen planus?
back: Purple, Polygonal, Papules, Pruritic, Plain-topped, Plaques.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is Wickham's striae in lichen planus?
back: A reticulate pattern seen on the surface of the lesion under magnification.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical distribution of lichen planus skin lesions?
back: The flexor surfaces.

subject: Dermatology
front: Is true Koebner's phenomenon positive or negative in lichen planus?
back: Positive.

subject: Dermatology
front: What proportion of lichen planus cases have oral cavity involvement, and how does it typically present?
back: 40-60% of cases; typically asymptomatic or with a burning sensation when eating spicy food.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the most common nail finding in lichen planus?
back: Thinning of the nail plate.

subject: Dermatology
front: What nail finding is characteristic (though less common) in lichen planus?
back: Pterygium of the nail.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of alopecia results from lichen planus involving the hair?
back: Scarring alopecia.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does hyperkeratosis represent in the histopathology of lichen planus?
back: Thickening of the stratum corneum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is 'wedge-shaped hypergranulosis' in lichen planus histopathology?
back: A characteristic thickening of the granular layer in a wedge shape.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are colloid/Civatte bodies in lichen planus, and what do they represent?
back: Eosinophilic bodies representing necrotic keratinocytes.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the 'Max-Joseph space' in lichen planus histopathology?
back: A cleft at the dermoepidermal junction (DEJ).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of infiltrate is seen at the DEJ in lichen planus?
back: A band-like lymphocytic infiltrate.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the appearance of the rete ridges in lichen planus histopathology.
back: Saw-toothed elongation of rete ridges.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for limited lichen planus disease?
back: Topical steroids.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for extensive lichen planus disease?
back: Systemic steroids.

subject: Dermatology
front: What two features together constitute 'interface dermatitis' as seen in lichen planus?
back: Basal cell degeneration and band-like lymphocytic infiltrates.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of eruption is pityriasis rosea?
back: An acute, self-limiting papulo-squamous eruption.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the etiological agent of pityriasis rosea?
back: Human herpesvirus-7 (HHV-7).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the first lesion in pityriasis rosea called?
back: The herald patch (or mother patch).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of scale is seen within the lesions of pityriasis rosea?
back: Collarette scale.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the distribution pattern of pityriasis rosea lesions.
back: They follow the lines of Langer, producing a 'Christmas tree' pattern.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of disease is pityriasis rubra pilaris?
back: A chronic, inflammatory papulo-squamous disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the characteristic follicular lesions in pityriasis rubra pilaris?
back: Follicular papules.

subject: Dermatology
front: What degree of erythroderma is seen in pityriasis rubra pilaris?
back: Erythroderma involving more than 90% of body surface area.

subject: Dermatology
front: What characteristic feature distinguishes pityriasis rubra pilaris from other erythrodermas?
back: Presence of 'islands of sparing.'

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the 'checkerboard pattern' seen on histopathology of pityriasis rubra pilaris?
back: Alternating horizontal and vertical orthokeratosis and parakeratosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two broad categories of vesiculo-bullous diseases?
back: Immunobullous and mechanobullous diseases.

subject: Dermatology
front: Immunobullous diseases are classified based on what feature?
back: The level of split (intra-epidermal vs sub-epidermal).

subject: Dermatology
front: Which immunobullous disease has an intra-epidermal (superficial) split?
back: Pemphigus.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which immunobullous diseases have a sub-epidermal (deep) split?
back: Pemphigoid, dermatitis herpetiformis, and chronic bullous disease of childhood (CBDC).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the mechanobullous category of blistering disease also called?
back: Epidermolysis bullosa.

subject: Dermatology
front: How are vesicles and bullae differentiated by size?
back: Vesicles are less than 1 cm; bullae are greater than 1 cm.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the word 'pemphigus' mean, and at what level does the bulla form?
back: It means blisters; the level of the bulla is intraepidermal.

subject: Dermatology
front: What structure is the target of autoantibodies in pemphigus, and what is the result of its rupture?
back: Desmosomes; rupture causes acantholysis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the target antigens in pemphigus?
back: Desmogleins, desmocollin, and plakins.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two main types of pemphigus?
back: Pemphigus foliaceus and pemphigus vulgaris.

subject: Dermatology
front: What mnemonic helps recall the target antigen of pemphigus foliaceus?
back: 'Foliaceus First' — targets desmoglein-1 (Dsg-1).

subject: Dermatology
front: Where is desmoglein-1 located in the epidermis?
back: In the upper epidermis, just below the stratum corneum.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the pathogenic sequence in pemphigus foliaceus?
back: IgG antibody forms against Dsg-1, causing acantholysis and subcorneal bullae with a very thin (flaccid) roof.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical bullae seen in pemphigus foliaceus.
back: Transient, superficial bullae that rupture very easily, leaving scales and crusts.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical distribution of pemphigus foliaceus lesions?
back: Seborrheic areas (scalp, face, chest, back).

subject: Dermatology
front: Is mucosal involvement seen in pemphigus foliaceus, and why or why not?
back: No mucosal involvement, due to low Dsg-1 levels in mucosa and loss of Dsg-1 being compensated by Dsg-3.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the histopathological split seen in pemphigus foliaceus?
back: A subcorneal split.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which is the more common type of pemphigus, foliaceus or vulgaris?
back: Pemphigus vulgaris.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the pathogenic sequence in pemphigus vulgaris?
back: IgG anti-Dsg-3 antibody forms, causing acantholysis and suprabasal bullae with a thin (flaccid) roof.

subject: Dermatology
front: Why are oral lesions seen in pemphigus vulgaris but not pemphigus foliaceus?
back: Because of Dsg-3 involvement, which is present in mucosal tissue.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the target antigens in the mucosal-dominant type of pemphigus vulgaris, and where is this antigen located?
back: Dsg-3, located in the lower epidermis, above the stratum basale.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the target antigens in the muco-cutaneous type of pemphigus vulgaris?
back: Dsg-3 and Dsg-1.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical age of onset for pemphigus vulgaris?
back: 40-60 years.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the bullae and erosions seen in pemphigus vulgaris.
back: Flaccid bullae that rupture easily, and erosions that are slow to heal and tend to extend.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe how the Nikolsky sign is elicited and what it indicates.
back: Tangential pressure is applied on normal-appearing skin; a positive sign shows separation of the upper from lower epidermal layers, indicating intra-epidermal loss of cohesion between keratinocytes.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe how the bullae spread sign is elicited and what it indicates.
back: Unidirectional finger pressure is applied over a bulla; a positive sign produces an irregular, angulated margin, indicating a flaccid bulla as seen in pemphigus vulgaris.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does Tzanck smear show in pemphigus?
back: Acantholytic cells (Tzanck cells).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is seen on histopathology (HPE) in pemphigus vulgaris regarding the split and basal layer?
back: A suprabasal split with a 'row of tombstone' appearance — basal keratinocytes remain attached to the basement membrane due to intact hemidesmosomes.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for pemphigus, and what does it show?
back: Direct immunofluorescence (DIF); it shows deposition of immunoreactants in the skin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the 'fish net pattern' seen on DIF in pemphigus?
back: Intraepidermal, intercellular deposition of IgG and C3.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the mainstay treatment for pemphigus?
back: High-dose systemic steroids.

subject: Dermatology
front: What biologic drug is used in pemphigus and what is its target?
back: Rituximab, which targets anti-CD20 on B cells.

subject: Dermatology
front: What immunosuppressant is used in pemphigus, and what is its notable side effect?
back: Cyclophosphamide; its notable side effect is hemorrhagic cystitis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What structure do autoantibodies target in bullous pemphigoid?
back: Hemidesmosomes, connecting the stratum basale to the basement membrane zone (BMZ).

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two antigens targeted in bullous pemphigoid, and which is more common?
back: BPAG-2 (180 kDa, more common) and BPAG-1 (230 kDa).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the pathogenic sequence leading to blister formation in bullous pemphigoid?
back: Complement activation and eosinophil recruitment cause release of proteolytic enzymes, which disrupt the basement membrane zone (BMZ).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the bullae seen in bullous pemphigoid.
back: Sub-epidermal, tense bullae with a thick roof.

subject: Dermatology
front: What drug classes can trigger bullous pemphigoid?
back: Gliptins and immune checkpoint inhibitors (e.g., pembrolizumab).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical age group affected by bullous pemphigoid?
back: 60-80 years.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical bullae of bullous pemphigoid and their tendency to heal.
back: Tense bullae on an erythematous/red base that rupture least readily, forming erosions that tend to heal without tendency to extend.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the typical sites of bullous pemphigoid?
back: Flexural aspects of the limbs, trunk, and abdomen.

subject: Dermatology
front: Is the Nikolsky sign positive or negative in bullous pemphigoid, and what is the bulla spread sign finding?
back: Nikolsky sign is negative; the bulla spread sign shows round, regular margins.

subject: Dermatology
front: What proportion of bullous pemphigoid cases have oral cavity involvement?

subject: Dermatology
front: What does Tzanck smear show in bullous pemphigoid?
back: Eosinophils.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does histopathology show in bullous pemphigoid?
back: A sub-epidermal blister containing eosinophils.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does DIF show in bullous pemphigoid?
back: Linear deposition of IgG and C3 at the basement membrane zone.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for bullous pemphigoid?
back: Low-dose corticosteroids.

subject: Dermatology
front: What class of autoantibody is seen in chronic bullous disease of childhood?
back: IgA.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the target antigen in chronic bullous disease of childhood?
back: LAD-1 (120 kDa), an extracellular fragment of BPAG-2.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical age group affected by chronic bullous disease of childhood?
back: Less than 5 years.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the level and type of deposit seen in chronic bullous disease of childhood?
back: Sub-epidermal, with neutrophilic deposits.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the lesions seen in chronic bullous disease of childhood.
back: Annular arrangement of vesicles, giving a 'cluster of jewels'/'string of pearls' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for chronic bullous disease of childhood?
back: Dapsone (a neutrophil chemotaxis inhibitor).

subject: Dermatology
front: What do the terms 'dermatitis' and 'herpetiformis' refer to in dermatitis herpetiformis?
back: 'Dermatitis' refers to intensely pruritic lesions; 'herpetiformis' refers to their grouped arrangement.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the autoantibody target in dermatitis herpetiformis?
back: IgA autoantibody to epidermal transglutaminase-3.

subject: Dermatology
front: At what level does the blister form in dermatitis herpetiformis?
back: Subepidermal.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which HLA types are associated with dermatitis herpetiformis?
back: HLA DQ2, DQ8, and B8.

subject: Dermatology
front: What systemic condition is dermatitis herpetiformis associated with?
back: Coeliac sprue/gluten-sensitive enteropathy.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical presentation of dermatitis herpetiformis.
back: Intensely pruritic papulovesicles that, when scratched, form grouped excoriations, with an extensor distribution over the elbows and knees.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does Tzanck smear show in dermatitis herpetiformis?
back: Neutrophils.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does DIF show in dermatitis herpetiformis?
back: Granular IgA deposits at the basement membrane zone and dermal papilla.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the screening serodiagnosis test for dermatitis herpetiformis/coeliac disease?
back: ELISA for anti-TG2 IgA (tissue transglutaminase antibody).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment of choice (TOC) for dermatitis herpetiformis?
back: A gluten-free diet, avoiding BROW (barley, rye, oats, and wheat).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the drug of choice (DOC) for dermatitis herpetiformis and its mechanism?
back: Dapsone, which inhibits neutrophil chemotaxis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the other name for mechano bullous disorder?
back: Epidermolysis bullosa.

subject: Dermatology
front: What causes bullous lesions in epidermolysis bullosa?
back: Mechanical trauma.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the defect in EB simplex, and at what level does it act?
back: Keratin 5/14 (K5/K14) defect; it is the superficial type.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the defect in junctional EB?
back: Laminin defect.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the defect in dystrophic EB, and at what level does it act?
back: Collagen-7 defect; it is the deep type.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical clinical history suggestive of epidermolysis bullosa?
back: A mother notices blisters on handling the baby, with blisters forming at sites of mechanical trauma.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is leprosy also called?
back: Hansen's disease (HD).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of disease is leprosy?
back: A chronic, infectious, granulomatous disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two causative organisms of leprosy?
back: Mycobacterium leprae and Mycobacterium lepromatosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of organism is Mycobacterium leprae?
back: An obligate intracellular acid-fast bacillus (AFB).

subject: Dermatology
front: What two tissues does M. leprae target?
back: Skin and nerve (Schwann cells).

subject: Dermatology
front: Which sensory modality is lost first in leprosy nerve involvement?
back: Thermal sensation (hot-cold discrimination).

subject: Dermatology
front: After exposure to M. leprae, what proportion of people mount excellent cell-mediated immunity (CMI), and what is the outcome?
back: 95%; the outcome is no disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: After exposure to M. leprae, what proportion of people have CMI that is 'not yet determined', and what do they develop?
back: 5%; they develop indeterminate HD.

subject: Dermatology
front: Of patients with indeterminate HD, what proportion progress to determinate HD, undergo spontaneous healing, and persist unchanged?
back: 25% progress to determinate HD, 70% undergo spontaneous healing, and 5% persist.

subject: Dermatology
front: What population is classically affected by indeterminate HD?
back: Children from endemic areas such as Bihar, UP, and Tamil Nadu.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the lesion of indeterminate HD.
back: An ill-defined hypopigmented macule.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the most common site of the indeterminate HD lesion?
back: The face.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the sensation status of the indeterminate HD lesion?
back: Usually normal, with minimum impairment.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is an important differential diagnosis for indeterminate HD, and how is it distinguished?
back: Pityriasis alba, which shows scaling (endogenous eczema), whereas indeterminate HD does not.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the biopsy finding in indeterminate HD?
back: Perineural and periappendageal lymphocytosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What classification system is used for the clinical spectrum of determinate leprosy?
back: The Ridley-Jopling classification: TT, BT, BB, BL, LL.

subject: Dermatology
front: Along the leprosy spectrum, how do AFB load and CMI change from limited to disseminated disease?
back: AFB load increases and CMI decreases as disease progresses from limited (TT) to disseminated (LL) disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare CMI to M. leprae in tuberculoid (TT) vs lepromatous (LL) leprosy.
back: TT: strong CMI; LL: poor CMI.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare granuloma formation in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: well-defined, localized granuloma; LL: ill-defined, diffuse granuloma with foamy macrophages.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the spread of M. leprae in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: limited spread; LL: uncontrolled multiplication.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare disease extent in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: localized disease; LL: disseminated disease.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the number of skin lesions in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: 1-3 lesions; LL: innumerable lesions.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the symmetry of skin lesions in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: asymmetrical; LL: symmetrical.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the border characteristics of lesions in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: well-defined border; LL: ill-defined border.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the pattern of nerve involvement in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: asymmetrical nerve involvement; LL: symmetrical, multiple nerve involvement.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare sensation/sweating over lesions in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: sensation/sweating is lost; LL: sensation/sweating is preserved in early stages.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the number of M. leprae organisms in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: few organisms; LL: large numbers of organisms.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the slit-skin smear result in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: negative; LL: positive.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare the lepromin test result in TT vs LL leprosy.
back: TT: positive; LL: negative.

subject: Dermatology
front: Is the lepromin test diagnostic or prognostic for leprosy?
back: It is prognostic only, not diagnostic.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the number of lesions and border character in tuberculoid (TT) leprosy.
back: 1-3 skin lesions, forming an annular plaque with central clearing and a well-defined margin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What associated features are seen with tuberculoid (TT) leprosy lesions?
back: Granuloma formation, anaesthesia, alopecia, and anhidrosis over the lesion.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the 'side profile' appearance of a TT leprosy plaque called?
back: Saucer right-way-up appearance.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the number and border character of lesions in borderline tuberculoid (BT) leprosy.
back: 3-10 lesions, with well-defined and not-so-well-defined margins present together, and satellite lesions.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is another name for mid-borderline (BB) leprosy, and what is its stability?
back: Dimorphous leprosy; it is the most unstable form of leprosy.

subject: Dermatology
front: What features are combined in mid-borderline (BB) leprosy?
back: Features of both TT and LL leprosy.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the lesions of mid-borderline (BB) leprosy.
back: 10-30 polymorphic lesions with a geographic map-like appearance.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the margin characteristics of mid-borderline (BB) leprosy lesions.
back: An inner punched-out margin (Swiss cheese appearance) and a sloping outer margin (inverted saucer appearance).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the lesions of borderline lepromatous (BL) leprosy.
back: More than 30 lesions, with skin lesions and nerve thickening that are almost symmetrical, showing an inverted saucer appearance.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the extent and systemic involvement of lepromatous (LL) leprosy.
back: Innumerable lesions with systemic disease affecting skin, nerve, and internal organs, with symmetric skin lesions and peripheral nerve thickening.

subject: Dermatology
front: What early autonomic feature can occur in lepromatous (LL) leprosy?
back: Epistaxis and pedal edema, due to autonomic nervous system dysfunction.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the late features of lepromatous (LL) leprosy?
back: Leonine facies (thick skin with furrows) and glove-and-stocking peripheral neuropathy.

subject: Dermatology
front: Name seven categories of nerve involvement and deformity seen in Hansen's disease.
back: Lateral madarosis, saddle nose, claw hand, foot drop, cranial nerve involvement, peripheral nerve thickening, and trophic/neuropathic ulcers.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which nerve is involved in producing a claw hand deformity in leprosy?
back: The ulnar nerve.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which nerve is involved in producing foot drop in leprosy?
back: The common peroneal nerve.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the sequence of events when cranial nerve V is affected in leprosy?
back: Decreased corneal sensation leads to exposure keratitis, then corneal ulcer, then corneal opacity.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which cranial nerve is most commonly involved in leprosy, and what deformity results?
back: CN VII (most common); it causes orbicularis oculi paralysis leading to lagophthalmos.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which peripheral nerve commonly shows thickening in leprosy, and where is it located anatomically?
back: The greater auricular nerve, which crosses the sternocleidomastoid muscle.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the pathophysiological sequence leading to trophic/neuropathic ulcers in leprosy.
back: Peripheral neuropathy causes decreased pain sensation, leading to repeated undetected injury and chronic ulceration at pressure points.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the three components of management for trophic/neuropathic ulcers in leprosy.
back: Rest with leg elevation (to decrease swelling), offloading devices (non-weight bearing splints), and microcellular rubber (MCR) footwear.

subject: Dermatology
front: How many cardinal features of Hansen's disease must be present for diagnosis?
back: At least 1 of 3 cardinal features.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the skin cardinal feature of Hansen's disease?
back: A hypopigmented or reddish lesion with definitive sensory loss.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the nerve cardinal feature of Hansen's disease?
back: An enlarged peripheral nerve with sensory/motor deficit in the area it supplies.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the third cardinal feature of Hansen's disease?
back: A positive slit-skin smear for M. leprae.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the 'Grenz zone' seen on histopathology of leprosy, and in which type is it absent?
back: A sub-epidermal free zone with no cellular infiltrate; it is absent (negative) in tuberculoid (TT) leprosy.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are 'lepra/Virchow cells' on histopathology, and what do they contain?
back: Foamy macrophages containing acid-fast bacilli (AFB) in diffuse sheets.

subject: Dermatology
front: What stain is used to demonstrate M. leprae on histopathology?
back: Fite Faraco stain.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the 'pure neuritic type' of leprosy?
back: A form where the skin appears normal and only the nerve is involved.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the investigation of choice (IOC) for pure neuritic leprosy, and which nerve is preferred?
back: Nerve biopsy, preferably of the best sensory nerve available.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which nerve is biopsied in the upper limb and lower limb for suspected pure neuritic leprosy?
back: Upper limb: radial cutaneous nerve; lower limb: sural nerve.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the name of the multidrug therapy (MDT) kit used for adults with leprosy?
back: The maroon kit, given over 28 days.

subject: Dermatology
front: How many skin lesions define paucibacillary (PB) leprosy, and how many define multibacillary (MB) leprosy?
back: PB: 1-5 skin lesions; MB: 6 or more skin lesions.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare nerve involvement in paucibacillary vs multibacillary leprosy.
back: PB: no nerves involved; MB: 1 or more nerves involved.

subject: Dermatology
front: Compare slit-skin smear (SSS) for AFB in paucibacillary vs multibacillary leprosy.
back: PB: negative; MB: positive.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the duration of MDT treatment for paucibacillary vs multibacillary leprosy?
back: PB: 6 months; MB: 12 months.

subject: Dermatology
front: How many drugs are used in MDT for both paucibacillary and multibacillary leprosy?
back: 3 drugs in each regimen.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the drug regimen (per 28 days) for paucibacillary leprosy?
back: Rifampicin 600 mg once a month, plus dapsone 100 mg daily from day 1 to day 28.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the drug regimen (per 28 days) for multibacillary leprosy?
back: Rifampicin 600 mg once a month, dapsone 100 mg daily (D1-D28), plus clofazimine 300 mg on day 1 and 50 mg daily from day 2 to day 28.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the adverse effects of rifampicin?
back: Reddish discoloration of secretions and hepatotoxicity.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the adverse effects of dapsone?
back: Hemolytic anemia, agranulocytosis, methemoglobinemia, hepatitis, and drug hypersensitivity syndrome.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the adverse effects of clofazimine?
back: Hyperpigmentation, ichthyosis, and GI disturbance.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is used for chemoprophylaxis of leprosy contacts?
back: A single dose of rifampicin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What age group is eligible for single dose rifampicin chemoprophylaxis?
back: Children aged 2 years and above, and adults.

subject: Dermatology
front: What must be ruled out before giving single-dose rifampicin chemoprophylaxis?
back: Existing leprosy or tuberculosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: According to WHO guidelines, what defines a 'contact' eligible for chemoprophylaxis?
back: Close proximity to a newly detected case for a prolonged duration — living, working, or social activities for at least 20 hours/week over 3 months in the past year.

subject: Dermatology
front: What vaccine is used for immunoprophylaxis of leprosy?
back: Mycobacterium indicus pranii (MIP) vaccine.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the three types of lepra reactions?
back: Type 1, Type 2, and the Lucio phenomenon (seen in Lucio leprosy, found in Mexico).

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of hypersensitivity reaction is Type 1 lepra reaction, and how does it present?
back: A Type 4 hypersensitivity reaction, presenting with existing lesions becoming red and tender, with severe neuritis (nerves markedly tender).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is another name for Type 2 lepra reaction, and what type of hypersensitivity is it?
back: Erythema nodosum leprosum; it is a Type 3 hypersensitivity reaction (immune complex disease).

subject: Dermatology
front: How does Type 2 lepra reaction present?
back: New, red, tender nodules with fever, and nerves may or may not be tender.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the drug of choice for both Type 1 and Type 2 lepra reactions, and is it safe in pregnancy?
back: Systemic steroids; yes, they can be used even in pregnancy.

subject: Dermatology
front: Should MDT for leprosy be continued during a lepra reaction?
back: Yes, MDT should always be continued.

subject: Dermatology
front: What additional drugs are used specifically for Type 2 lepra reaction?
back: Chloroquine, thalidomide, and a higher dose of clofazimine.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the mechanism of action of thalidomide in Type 2 lepra reaction, and what is its major adverse effect?
back: It inhibits TNF-alpha; its major adverse effect is teratogenicity.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for a nerve abscess in leprosy?
back: Incision and drainage, plus systemic steroids, plus continued MDT.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does NLEP stand for?
back: National Leprosy Eradication Programme.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the lotus in the NLEP emblem symbolize?
back: Beauty and purity.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the partially affected thumb in the NLEP emblem symbolize?
back: That leprosy can be cured and the patient can be a useful member of society.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the rising sun in the NLEP emblem symbolize?
back: Hope and optimism.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is SAPNA in the context of NLEP?
back: The mascot for NLEP in India.

subject: Dermatology
front: What does the SAPNA mascot represent?
back: A girl child who spreads awareness about leprosy in rural areas and villages.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the tagline associated with SAPNA?
back: 'Kyunki Sapna hai kusht mukt Bharat ka.'

subject: Dermatology
front: What is another name for Tuberculosis Verrucosa Cutis?
back: Anatomist's wart, or warty TB.

subject: Dermatology
front: In whom is Tuberculosis Verrucosa Cutis classically seen?
back: Those who prepare dissections and autopsies.

subject: Dermatology
front: Is Tuberculosis Verrucosa Cutis a paucibacillary or multibacillary form, and what is its mechanism?
back: A paucibacillary (PB) form, resulting from exogenous reinfection with M. tuberculosis in previously sensitized individuals with moderate-high immunity.

subject: Dermatology
front: What type of cutaneous TB is lupus vulgaris, and what is its course?
back: A chronic, progressive, paucibacillary (PB) form of cutaneous TB.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the most common cutaneous TB seen in adults?
back: Lupus vulgaris.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the lesion of lupus vulgaris.
back: An asymptomatic plaque with atrophy and scarring at the centre, and extension with an active, advancing margin at the periphery.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the typical sites of lupus vulgaris?
back: Face, buttocks, and extremities.

subject: Dermatology
front: What diascopy finding is seen in lupus vulgaris, and what does it represent?
back: Apple jelly nodules (yellow-brown), indicating the presence of dermal granulomas.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the investigation of choice for lupus vulgaris, and what does it show?
back: Skin biopsy for histopathology; it shows well-formed tuberculoid granulomas with Langhans giant cells in the dermis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the utility of PCR in diagnosing cutaneous TB?
back: It is a reliable confirmatory test in paucibacillary forms, detecting M. tuberculosis DNA with rapid diagnosis and higher specificity/sensitivity in smear-negative or culture-negative cases.

subject: Dermatology
front: Why is chest X-ray done in patients with cutaneous TB?
back: To screen for pulmonary TB, since 10-20% may have concurrent pulmonary tuberculosis.

subject: Dermatology
front: Is scrofuloderma a paucibacillary or multibacillary form, and in whom is it most commonly seen?
back: A multibacillary (MB) form; it is the most common cutaneous TB seen in children.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the pathogenic mechanism of scrofuloderma?
back: M. tuberculosis in a lymph node, bone, or fascia directly invades the overlying skin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the most common focus of infection in scrofuloderma?
back: Cervical lymph nodes.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features of scrofuloderma.
back: Painless subcutaneous nodules with discharging sinuses and ulcers with undermined edges and fibrotic scars.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is 'swimming pool granuloma' also called?
back: Fish tank granuloma.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the causative organism of swimming pool granuloma, and how is it acquired?
back: Mycobacterium marinum, acquired from contaminated fresh or salt water.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the skin lesion of swimming pool granuloma.
back: Nodular lymphangitis at the extremity.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for swimming pool granuloma?
back: Drugs of the tetracycline group.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes Buruli ulcer, and what toxin does it produce?
back: Mycobacterium ulcerans, which produces the toxin mycolactone, leading to ulceration.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the skin lesion of Buruli ulcer.
back: A large, painless ulcer with undermined edges.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for Buruli ulcer?
back: Rifampin plus streptomycin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the two broad types of pyoderma?
back: Follicular and non-follicular pyoderma.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the causative organism of follicular pyoderma?
back: Staphylococcus aureus.

subject: Dermatology
front: What structure is infected in folliculitis, and describe the clinical feature.
back: The hair follicle; presents as a follicle-oriented pustule.

subject: Dermatology
front: What structures are infected in a furuncle (boil), and describe the clinical feature.
back: The hair follicle and perifollicular area; presents as a red, indurated, tender nodule.

subject: Dermatology
front: What structures are infected in a carbuncle, and what systemic disease is it associated with?
back: Multiple hair follicles and contiguous areas; associated with diabetes mellitus.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features and typical site of a carbuncle.
back: A red, indurated, tender nodule with pustules on the surface, most commonly at the nape of the neck.

subject: Dermatology
front: How is non-follicular pyoderma broadly classified?
back: As localized or spreading.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the superficial and deep forms of localized non-follicular pyoderma?
back: Superficial: impetigo (bacterial infection of the epidermis); deep: ecthyma.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the superficial and deep forms of spreading non-follicular pyoderma?
back: Superficial: erysipelas; deep: cellulitis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organisms cause non-bullous impetigo (impetigo contagiosa)?
back: Staphylococcus aureus and Group A Streptococcus.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features of non-bullous impetigo and its typical population.
back: Occurs in children, presenting with golden-brown, honey-colored crusting.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical site of non-bullous impetigo?
back: The face (perinasal and perioral areas).

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes bullous impetigo?
back: Staphylococcus aureus.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features of bullous impetigo.
back: Bullae present due to toxin production, with a varnish-like crust.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the typical site of bullous impetigo?
back: The trunk.

subject: Dermatology
front: Name the topical antibacterial agents used in pyoderma.
back: Fusidic acid, mupirocin, and ozenoxacin.

subject: Dermatology
front: Which topical antibacterials are particularly effective against MRSA?
back: Mupirocin and ozenoxacin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes erysipelas, and at what level of skin does it occur?
back: Group A Streptococcus; it involves the upper half of the dermis and lymphatics.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the border characteristics of erysipelas.
back: Well-demarcated and raised.

subject: Dermatology
