front: What organisms cause cellulitis, and at what level of skin does it occur?
back: Group A Streptococcus/Staphylococcus aureus; it involves the lower half of the dermis and subcutaneous tissue.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the border characteristics of cellulitis.
back: Ill-demarcated and diffuse.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the pathogenic sequence of Staphylococcal Scalded Skin Syndrome (SSSS).
back: A distant source of infection (e.g. ear or conjunctiva) releases an epidermolytic exotoxin that diffuses through the blood, reaching the skin where it targets desmoglein-1, causing a subcorneal split due to acantholysis.

subject: Dermatology
front: List the clinical features of SSSS.
back: Fever (and other constitutional symptoms), severe skin tenderness, and sheet-like epidermal peeling.

subject: Dermatology
front: Are oral lesions present in SSSS, and why or why not?
back: No, because desmoglein-3 is not affected.

subject: Dermatology
front: What causes acute paronychia?
back: Nail fold infection, typically by Staphylococcus aureus.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features of acute paronychia.
back: Erythema with collection of pus.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for acute paronychia?
back: Anti-staphylococcal antibiotics, plus incision and drainage if frank pus is present.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes erythrasma?
back: Corynebacterium minutissimum.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical presentation of erythrasma.
back: Asymptomatic reddish-brown hyperpigmentation, typically in the axilla and groin.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the Wood's lamp finding in erythrasma?
back: Coral red fluorescence.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes trichomycosis axillaris, and is it a fungal infection?
back: Corynebacterium tenuis; despite its name, it is not a fungal infection (a misnomer).

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical finding in trichomycosis axillaris.
back: Yellow concretions on axillary hair shafts.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes pitted keratolysis?
back: Micrococcus/Kytococcus sedentarius.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical finding in pitted keratolysis.
back: Multiple punctate depressions on the palms and soles, due to keratolysis.

subject: Dermatology
front: List three cutaneous conditions caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa.
back: Ecthyma gangrenosum, hot tub folliculitis, and green nail syndrome.

subject: Dermatology
front: What causes the green color in green nail syndrome?
back: A pigment produced by Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes meningococcemia?
back: Neisseria meningitidis.

subject: Dermatology
front: What are the clinical features of meningococcemia?
back: Fever with signs of meningitis (e.g., neck rigidity), along with a non-blanchable petechial rash.

subject: Dermatology
front: What organism causes cutaneous anthrax, and what occupational exposure is relevant?
back: Bacillus anthracis; relevant occupational history is contact with animal products.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the sequence of lesion evolution in cutaneous anthrax (malignant pustule).
back: A central ulcer forms, followed by a black scab, with surrounding edema.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the McFadyean reaction used to diagnose, and how is it performed?
back: Cutaneous anthrax; tissue smears are stained with polychrome methylene blue, showing purplish material (capsule) around blue-stained bacilli.

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the causative organism and vector of scrub typhus?
back: Orientia tsutsugamushi, transmitted by the trombiculid mite.

subject: Dermatology
front: What history is relevant in scrub typhus?
back: A history of travel to a forest area.

subject: Dermatology
front: Describe the clinical features of scrub typhus.
back: Fever, CNS symptoms, and an eschar (a black scab surrounded by erythema).

subject: Dermatology
front: What is the treatment for scrub typhus?
back: Doxycycline.

subject: Dermatology
front: List three differential diagnoses for a skin eschar.
back: Scrub typhus, cutaneous anthrax, and brown recluse spider bite.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What type of genetic material do herpes viruses have?
back: DNA.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is orolabial herpes also called, and what virus causes it?
back: Fever blister/cold sore; caused by HSV-1.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the lesion of orolabial herpes.
back: Vesicles in a grouped pattern on the lip margin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What does Tzanck smear show in orolabial herpes?
back: Multinucleated giant cells (a cytopathic effect).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment for orolabial herpes?
back: Acyclovir 400 mg three times daily (TID) for 5 days.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What virus causes varicella (chickenpox)?
back: Varicella-zoster virus (VZV).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Until when is a patient with varicella considered infectious?
back: Until all lesions have crusted.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What symptom typically precedes the rash in varicella?
back: Low-grade fever.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the classic appearance of varicella vesicles.
back: Vesicles on a red base, described as a 'dewdrop on a rose petal' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the distribution pattern of varicella lesions.
back: They appear first on the trunk, following a centripetal distribution.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What term describes the varying stages of lesions seen together in varicella?
back: A pleomorphic rash.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the pathogenic mechanism of herpes zoster (shingles)?
back: Reactivation of VZV within the dorsal root ganglion (a sensory structure).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the pre-eruptive phase of herpes zoster.
back: Localised burning, tingling, itching, or shooting pain.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the eruptive phase of herpes zoster.
back: Unilateral, dermatomal distribution (most commonly thoracic), with painful grouped vesicles on a red base.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the most common dermatome involved in herpes zoster?
back: The thoracic dermatome.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the major complication of herpes zoster?
back: Post-herpetic neuralgia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Compare the bioavailability and relative use of acyclovir vs valacyclovir for herpes zoster.
back: Valacyclovir has increased bioavailability compared to acyclovir.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the mechanism of action of bortezomib, and what is it used to treat?
back: It is a proteasome inhibitor used to treat multiple myeloma.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What adverse viral reactivation can bortezomib cause?
back: It can reactivate VZV, causing herpes zoster.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What virus causes oral hairy leukoplakia, and with what condition is it associated?
back: Epstein-Barr virus (EBV); associated with HIV-infected patients.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the lesion of oral hairy leukoplakia.
back: A white, corrugated plaque with furrows/ridges that cannot be rubbed off.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What virus causes Kaposi sarcoma?
back: Human herpesvirus-8 (HHV-8).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the clinical appearance of Kaposi sarcoma lesions.
back: Purplish plaques/nodules.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the significance of Kaposi sarcoma in HIV/AIDS, and what type of tumor is it?
back: It is an AIDS-defining illness and a low-grade vascular tumor.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What viruses cause molluscum contagiosum, and to which virus family do they belong?
back: MCV-1 (in children) and MCV-2 (in adults), belonging to the poxvirus family (DNA viruses).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the characteristic lesion of molluscum contagiosum in children.
back: Pearly white, dome-shaped, centrally umbilicated papules.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What phenomenon causes auto-inoculation and spread of lesions in molluscum contagiosum?
back: Pseudo-Koebner's phenomenon.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Is facial involvement seen in childhood molluscum contagiosum?
back: Yes, facial involvement is present.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the typical site and population affected by molluscum contagiosum in adults?
back: Anogenital involvement (most common), occurring in sexually active adults.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the first histopathological feature of molluscum contagiosum.
back: A cup-shaped invagination of the epidermis into the dermis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What are Henderson-Patterson bodies, as seen in molluscum contagiosum histopathology?
back: Eosinophilic, intracytoplasmic inclusions.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Is molluscum contagiosum self-limiting, and what physical treatment method is used?
back: Yes, it is self-limiting; curettage is the physical treatment method.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What topical drug is used for molluscum contagiosum, and what is its mechanism?
back: Imiquimod, an immunomodulator that acts as a TLR-7 agonist.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: In an HIV patient with molluscum-like lesions confined to skin only, what is the likely diagnosis?
back: Disseminated molluscum contagiosum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: In an HIV patient with molluscum-like lesions in skin plus lungs, what is the likely diagnosis?
back: Disseminated histoplasmosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: In an HIV patient with molluscum-like lesions in skin plus CNS, what is the likely diagnosis?
back: Disseminated cryptococcosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is notable about monkeypox (Mpox) as an emerging disease, and what symptom precedes its rash?
back: It is an emerging sexually transmitted disease; high-grade fever precedes rash onset.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What additional systemic finding accompanies monkeypox (Mpox)?
back: Lymphadenopathy.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the characteristic lesions of monkeypox (Mpox).
back: Large, deep-seated vesicles/bullae with central umbilication.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is verruca vulgaris also called, and what HPV types cause it?
back: Common warts; caused by HPV types 2, 4, and 27.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the clinical appearance of verruca vulgaris.
back: Asymptomatic, verrucous papules/plaques.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the topical treatment for verruca vulgaris, and what physical treatment can also be used?
back: Topical salicylic acid (a keratolytic); electrocautery can also be used.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What viruses cause hand-foot-mouth disease (HFMD)?
back: Coxsackievirus A16 and Enterovirus 71.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the lesions of HFMD and their sites.
back: Vesicular lesions on the palms, soles, buttocks, and oral cavity.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Name five viral causes of vesicular rashes.
back: HSV-1, HSV-2, VZV, monkeypox (Mpox), and hand-foot-mouth disease (HFMD).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is unique about Parvovirus B19 among DNA viruses?
back: It is the only single-stranded DNA virus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is another name for fifth disease caused by Parvovirus B19?
back: Erythema infectiosum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the facial rash of fifth disease/erythema infectiosum.
back: Malar erythema, giving a 'slapped cheek' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the truncal rash of fifth disease/erythema infectiosum.
back: A reticulate/net-like rash.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is Papular Purpuric Glove and Socks Syndrome (PPGS), and what virus causes it?
back: A Parvovirus B19-related condition presenting with symmetrical, painful erythema plus petechiae/purpura affecting the palms and soles.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organism most commonly causes pityriasis versicolor?
back: Malassezia globosa (more common than M. furfur).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the most common site of pityriasis versicolor?
back: The chest/trunk.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the scale type seen in pityriasis versicolor.
back: Fine, branny, furfuraceous scales.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the 10% KOH mount finding in pityriasis versicolor, and what does each component represent?
back: The 'spaghetti and meatball' appearance — spaghetti represents short hyphae and meatball represents round spores.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the Wood's lamp finding in pityriasis versicolor?
back: Yellow fluorescence.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the first-line treatment for pityriasis versicolor?
back: Topical azoles or selenium sulphide.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the second-line treatment for pityriasis versicolor?
back: Fluconazole.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe oral candidiasis (oral thrush) and its association.
back: White pseudomembranous plaques that can be rubbed off; associated with HIV infection.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is 'atrophic candidiasis'?
back: A form of oral candidiasis presenting with red plaques instead of white pseudomembranes.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe candidal intertrigo and where it occurs.
back: Involves intertriginous areas (skin folds); shows a red, moist macerated plaque with peripheral satellite pustules.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe candidal balanoposthitis and its systemic association.
back: Presents with radial fissuring of the prepuce; associated with diabetes mellitus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment for candidiasis?
back: Fluconazole (systemic) or clotrimazole (topical).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is dermatophytosis/tinea infection?
back: A superficial infection of keratinized tissue caused by dermatophytes.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What does 'tinea capitis' mean, and how is it classified?
back: Involvement of scalp hair; classified as non-inflammatory or inflammatory.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the endothrix pattern of non-inflammatory tinea capitis.
back: Black dots with multiple broken scalp hairs.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the ectothrix pattern of non-inflammatory tinea capitis.
back: Grey patches occurring in a child with patchy alopecia, with grey, scaly lesions.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe favus (inflammatory tinea capitis) and its typical scarring outcome.
back: Occurs in a child with scarring alopecia, showing scutula (yellow, cup-shaped crusts).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe kerion (inflammatory tinea capitis) and its typical scarring outcome.
back: Occurs in a child with scarring alopecia, boggy swelling, easily pluckable hair, positive regional lymphadenopathy, and a history of exposure to a pet dog.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is another name for tinea cruris, and describe its site and appearance.
back: Dhobi itch/jock itch; affects the groin with a prominent, scaly, erythematous border.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is another name for tinea pedis, and what is its typical site?
back: Athlete's foot; typical site is the 4th toe webspace.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the nail plate and subungual findings in onychomycosis.
back: Yellowish nail plate with subungual hyperkeratosis and tunnel formation.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the most common type of onychomycosis?
back: Distal lateral subungual onychomycosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Are nail pits present or absent in onychomycosis?
back: Absent (negative).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What are nail lacquers, and how do they work in onychomycosis treatment?
back: Topical formulations (e.g., amorolfine 5%, ciclopiroxolamine 8%) that form a film over the nail plate, allowing slow drug release and increased nail penetration.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is tavaborole, and what is its mechanism of action in onychomycosis?
back: A boron-derivative topical nail solution (5%) that inhibits leucyl tRNA synthetase, thereby inhibiting fungal protein synthesis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Name another topical nail solution used for onychomycosis besides tavaborole.
back: Efinaconazole 10%.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organisms cause tinea corporis?
back: Trichophyton mentagrophytes and T. rubrum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the clinical appearance of tinea corporis.
back: An annular plaque with central clearing and a raised, scaly margin, associated with itching.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is 'tinea incognito'?
back: Steroid-modified tinea, characterized by diffuse scaling and hypopigmentation/stretch marks due to steroid use.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Name the imidazole antifungals used for dermatophytoses.
back: Ketoconazole, luliconazole, and clotrimazole.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Name the triazole antifungals used for dermatophytoses.
back: Fluconazole and itraconazole.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Which allylamine is used for dermatophytoses, and what causes resistance to it among dermatophytes?
back: Terbinafine; resistance occurs due to mutations in squalene epoxidase.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organism is responsible for the epidemic of severe, recalcitrant dermatophytosis across the Indian subcontinent?
back: Trichophyton indotineae (formerly known as T. mentagrophyte genotype VIII).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is another name for subcutaneous mycoses, and why?
back: Implantation mycoses, due to traumatic inoculation of the fungus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organism causes sporotrichosis, and what type of fungus is it?
back: Sporothrix schenckii, a dimorphic fungus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is sporotrichosis also called, and what is the classic history?
back: Rose gardener's disease; classic history is a gardener with a history of thorn prick.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the lesion progression in sporotrichosis.
back: Nodulo-ulcerative lesions undergo proximal extension in a linear pattern along the lymphatics of the extremities.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment for sporotrichosis?
back: Itraconazole.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What histopathological finding is characteristic of sporotrichosis?
back: The asteroid body.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: List four differential diagnoses for sporotrichosis.
back: Nocardiosis, atypical mycobacterial infection (swimming pool/fish tank granuloma caused by M. marinum), cutaneous leishmaniasis, and tularemia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What type of fungi cause chromoblastomycosis, and what pigment do they contain?
back: Pigmented (dematiaceous) fungi, which contain melanin in their cell wall.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Name two organisms causing chromoblastomycosis and their reservoir.
back: Fonsecaea pedrosoi and Phialophora verrucosum, found in soil and decaying matter.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the typical history associated with chromoblastomycosis?
back: A farmer with a history of vegetable trauma.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the lesions of chromoblastomycosis.
back: Verrucous plaques with black dots on the surface, giving a cauliflower-like appearance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the 10% KOH mount finding in chromoblastomycosis, and what is it called?
back: Brown, round, thick-walled bodies, called medlar/sclerotic/copper penny bodies.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment for chromoblastomycosis?
back: Itraconazole.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organism causes fungal mycetoma (eumycetoma)?
back: Madurella species.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Name three organisms that cause bacterial mycetoma (actinomycetoma).
back: Actinomadura madurae, Nocardia species, and Streptomyces species.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the clinical triad of mycetoma?
back: Tumefaction (tumor-like swelling), discharging sinuses, and granules (colonies of microbes).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What color are the granules in fungal mycetoma (eumycetoma)?
back: Black granules.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the direct microscopy findings for fungal mycetoma (eumycetoma).
back: Gram-negative, septate hyphae, 2-5 micrometers in size.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the direct microscopy findings for bacterial mycetoma (actinomycetoma).
back: Gram-positive filaments, 0.5-1 micrometer in size.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment for fungal mycetoma (eumycetoma)?
back: Antifungals.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment regimen for bacterial mycetoma (actinomycetoma), and what is it called?
back: The Welsh regimen: amikacin plus cotrimoxazole.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What are the alternate names for old world cutaneous leishmaniasis?
back: Delhi boil, or oriental sore.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organisms cause old world cutaneous leishmaniasis, and what is the vector?
back: Leishmania major and L. tropica, transmitted by the Phlebotomus sandfly.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the clinical features of old world cutaneous leishmaniasis.
back: A nodulo-ulcerative lesion with a central crust.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the 'volcano sign' in cutaneous leishmaniasis?
back: An ulcer with an elevated rim, revealed after removal of the central crust.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the vector for new world cutaneous leishmaniasis?
back: The Lutzomyia sandfly.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is Post Kala-Azar Dermal Leishmaniasis (PKDL)?
back: A cutaneous sequela occurring secondary to Kala-azar (visceral leishmaniasis).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the typical history for PKDL?
back: A resident of Bihar with a past history of prolonged fever.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the skin lesions of PKDL on the trunk and face.
back: Trunk: hypopigmented macules; face: infiltrated nodules.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is an important differential diagnosis for PKDL, and how is it distinguished?
back: Lepromatous leprosy, which shows symmetric peripheral nerve thickening and a positive slit-skin smear for M. leprae.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What investigation and stain are used to diagnose leishmaniasis, and what is seen?
back: Giemsa stain, showing LD bodies (amastigote forms).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the drug of choice for Kala-azar (visceral leishmaniasis)?
back: Liposomal amphotericin B.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the drug of choice for PKDL, and by what route is it given?
back: Miltefosine, an oral drug.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organism causes scabies, and how many mites are typically present per patient?
back: Sarcoptes scabiei var hominis; typically 10-12 mites per patient.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What are the key history features of scabies?
back: Nocturnal pruritus and similar complaints among contacts.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Where are excoriated papules typically found in scabies?
back: In the web spaces.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the special/pathognomonic lesion of scabies, and where is it located?
back: Burrows, located in the stratum corneum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Why is the face spared in adult scabies?
back: Due to increased sebum activity in adults.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What investigation is used to confirm scabies?
back: KOH mount.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the first-line treatment for scabies, and in which populations can it be used?
back: Permethrin 5%; it can be used in infants ≥2 months, children, adults, and pregnant or lactating individuals, and is the safest and most effective option.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the FDA age/weight criterion for ivermectin use in scabies, and what is its main risk?
back: Greater than 15 kg or older than 5 years; the main risk is neurotoxicity due to an immature blood-brain barrier in young children.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Why is lindane contraindicated in infants and children under 10 years?
back: Due to risk of neurotoxicity and seizures.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the safety profile and action of crotamiton 10% cream/lotion?
back: It is safe in infants under medical supervision, and acts as both an antipruritic and a scabicidal agent.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What concentration of benzyl benzoate emulsion is used in adults for scabies treatment?
back: 25% (from the available range of 10-25%).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: In which age group is topical sulfur used for scabies, and what is its action?
back: Infants less than 2 months; it acts as a keratolytic.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: In which population is spinosad topical 0.9% suspension used for scabies?
back: Adults and children aged 4 years and above.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the underlying reason for facial involvement in infantile scabies?
back: Underdeveloped sebaceous glands in infants.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Where are vesicles typically seen in infantile scabies?
back: Over the palms and soles.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is Norwegian/crusted scabies, and how severe is it?
back: It is the most severe variant of scabies.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What conditions predispose to Norwegian/crusted scabies?
back: Immunosuppression (HIV, lepromatous leprosy) and Down's syndrome.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the paradox of pruritus and mite burden in Norwegian/crusted scabies.
back: There is minimal pruritis despite millions of mites being present.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment regimen for Norwegian/crusted scabies?
back: Ivermectin plus permethrin 5% plus a keratolytic (salicylic acid).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is Pediculosis corporis also called, and in what population is it most common?
back: Vagabond's disease; most common in individuals with decreased personal hygiene.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the location of the body/clothing louse and the resulting skin lesions.
back: The louse is found in the seams of clothing and feeds on the host, causing excoriations called 'morbus errorum.'

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the skin lesion (macula cerulea) seen in phthiriasis pubis, and its cause.
back: A blue macule caused by the anticoagulant nature of the louse's saliva.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What louse causes phthiriasis pubis, and what is its typical site?
back: The pubic louse (crab louse); typical site is the lower abdomen.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is Pediculosis capitis?
back: Infestation by the head louse.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What organisms cause cutaneous larva migrans?
back: Animal hookworm larvae — Ancylostoma braziliense and Ancylostoma caninum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the typical history in cutaneous larva migrans?
back: Walking barefoot at the beach.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: Describe the clinical feature and rate of tract progression in cutaneous larva migrans.
back: A migratory serpiginous tract, progressing at a speed of 1-2 cm/day.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is the treatment for cutaneous larva migrans?
back: Albendazole or ivermectin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What is 'larva currens' and what organism causes it?
back: A differential diagnosis for cutaneous larva migrans caused by Strongyloides stercoralis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: How does the speed of larval migration differ between larva currens and cutaneous larva migrans?
back: Larva currens progresses much faster, at 5-15 cm/hour ('racing larvae'), compared to 1-2 cm/day in cutaneous larva migrans.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-4: Viral, Fungal, Protozoal, Arthropod & Parasitic Infections
front: What additional skin finding may accompany larva currens?
back: An urticarial rash.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What causes anogenital warts, and what is their alternate name?
back: HPV-induced benign proliferations, also called condyloma acuminata.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What do the terms 'condyloma' and 'acuminata' mean?
back: Condyloma means round swelling; acuminata means pointed lesion.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which HPV types are low risk and cause 90% of anogenital warts?
back: HPV-6 and HPV-11.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which HPV types are high risk and cause 10% of anogenital warts?
back: HPV-16 and HPV-18.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the two clinical morphologies of anogenital warts?
back: Cauliflower-like lesions and pink, pointed papules/plaques (asymptomatic, fleshy).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is a koilocyte, as seen in the histopathology of anogenital warts?
back: A squamous epithelial cell with a hyperchromatic nucleus and a perinuclear halo.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the mechanism of action of podophyllin in treating anogenital warts?
back: It targets the mitotic spindle, causing wart necrosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the mechanism of action of imiquimod in treating anogenital warts, and in whom is it used?
back: A TLR-7 agonist; used in non-pregnant patients.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment of choice for anogenital warts in a pregnant patient?
back: Cryotherapy with liquid nitrogen at -196°C, which is also first-line therapy.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What concentration of trichloroacetic acid (TCA) is used to treat anogenital warts?

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How can transmission of anogenital warts be prevented?
back: By avoiding sexual intercourse when active lesions are present.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What organism causes gonococcal urethritis, and what is the incubation period?
back: Neisseria gonorrhoeae; incubation period is 2-5 days.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the discharge seen in gonococcal urethritis.
back: Profuse, purulent urethral discharge.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the microscopic finding in gonococcal urethritis, and what abbreviation is used?
back: Gram-negative, intracellular diplococci (GNICDC), with more intracellular than extracellular organisms.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment for gonococcal urethritis?
back: Injection ceftriaxone 500 mg IM, plus doxycycline 100 mg BD for 1 week if chlamydia is not excluded.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: List the organisms causing non-gonococcal urethritis, and which is most common.
back: Chlamydia trachomatis serovars D-K (most common), Ureaplasma urealyticum, Mycoplasma genitalium, and Trichomonas vaginalis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the discharge and incubation period of non-gonococcal urethritis.
back: Scanty, mucoid discharge; incubation period is 7-14 days.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the microscopic finding in non-gonococcal urethritis?
back: Numerous PMNs with absence of gram-negative intracellular diplococci (GNICDC).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment for non-gonococcal urethritis?
back: Azithromycin 1 g single dose, or doxycycline 100 mg BD for 1 week.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the culture medium used for Neisseria gonorrhoeae called, and what is its base medium?
back: Modified Thayer-Martin medium; the base medium is chocolate agar.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What antibiotic in modified Thayer-Martin medium inhibits gram-positive organisms?
back: Vancomycin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What antibiotic in modified Thayer-Martin medium inhibits gram-negative organisms (except Neisseria)?
back: Colistin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What antibiotic in modified Thayer-Martin medium inhibits fungal growth?
back: Nystatin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What antibiotic in modified Thayer-Martin medium inhibits Proteus growth?
back: Trimethoprim.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What test is used to diagnose chlamydia?
back: NAAT (nucleic acid amplification test).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What drug classes is Neisseria gonorrhoeae showing increasing resistance to?
back: Penicillin, quinolones, and macrolides (including azithromycin).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is disseminated gonococcal infection, and how frequently does it occur?
back: Bacteremic spread of gonococcus, occurring in 0.5-3% of cases.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What complement defect predisposes to disseminated gonococcal infection?
back: Deficiency of the terminal complement components (C5-C9), also known as the membrane attack complex.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the clinical triad of disseminated gonococcal infection?
back: Tenosynovitis, dermatitis, and polyarthralgia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the skin lesion of disseminated gonococcal infection.
back: Acral hemorrhagic vesiculopustular lesions.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What organism causes vaginal candidiasis, and describe the discharge.
back: Candida albicans; the discharge is curdy white.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What culture medium is used to differentiate species of Candida?
back: CHROM agar.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What symptom is prominent in candidiasis, and what drug class is it associated with?
back: Marked pruritus; associated with SGLT-2 inhibitors (e.g., dapagliflozin, canagliflozin).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What organism causes trichomoniasis, and describe the discharge.
back: Trichomonas vaginalis (a flagellate); the discharge is greenish-yellow and frothy.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What special cervical finding is seen in trichomoniasis?
back: Multiple punctate hemorrhages, giving a 'strawberry cervix' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What causes bacterial vaginosis, and describe the discharge.
back: Altered vaginal microflora, including Gardnerella and Mobiluncus; the discharge is homogenous, white, and adherent.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the whiff test/amine test in bacterial vaginosis, and what is a positive result?
back: Vaginal discharge mixed with KOH produces a fishy odour.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are clue cells, and what percentage is required for diagnosis of bacterial vaginosis?
back: Squamous epithelial cells covered with surface bacteria; more than 20% of cells being clue cells supports diagnosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the Nugent score used for?
back: A Gram stain-based scoring system for vaginal swabs, diagnostic of bacterial vaginosis if the score is 7-10.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the diagnosis and treatment for any case of cervical erosion/ulcer/mucopurulent discharge?
back: Diagnosis is cervicitis; treatment is the grey kit.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What organism causes syphilis?
back: Treponema pallidum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe early congenital syphilis (age ≤2 years).
back: Presents with vesiculobullous lesions, called syphilitic pemphigus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Hutchinson's triad, seen in late congenital syphilis (age >2 years)?
back: Interstitial keratitis, Hutchinson's teeth (notched upper central incisors), and sensorineural hearing loss (SNHL).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the Diday-Kassowitz law?
back: In an untreated syphilitic mother, as pregnancies progress, infectivity decreases and immunity increases, so the likelihood of fetal infection decreases in subsequent pregnancies (improved outcome).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the incubation period of primary syphilis?
back: 9-90 days.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the hard chancre of primary syphilis.
back: A single, clean-based, indurated, non-tender lesion that does not bleed on touch.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the inguinal lymph nodes in primary syphilis.
back: Bilaterally enlarged, non-tender, and rubbery.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the investigation of choice for primary syphilis, and what is characteristic of the organism seen?
back: Dark ground microscopy; the slender organism shows corkscrew motility.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How does secondary syphilis spread, and what are its general clinical features?
back: It spreads hematogenously, with constitutional symptoms and generalized lymphadenopathy.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are condyloma lata in secondary syphilis?
back: Flat-topped, moist mucosal lesions in the anogenital region.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the alopecia pattern of secondary syphilis and its scarring status.
back: A moth-eaten pattern, which is non-scarring.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the characteristic rash of secondary syphilis.
back: Symmetric, non-pruritic, polymorphic rash without vesicles or bullae, involving the palms and soles.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the Buschke-Ollendorff sign, and what causes it?
back: Deep dermal tenderness elicited when skin lesions are pressed with a blunt object, due to endarteritis obliterans.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the investigation of choice for secondary syphilis?
back: VDRL/RPR (rapid plasma reagin) test.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is a gumma in gummatous (tertiary) syphilis?
back: A hard, rubbery nodule that breaks open to form an ulcer.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What cardiovascular complications occur in tertiary syphilis, and what is the most common aortic segment involved?
back: Aortic aneurysms and aortic regurgitation; the ascending aorta is the most commonly involved segment.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is general paresis of the insane (GPI) in neurosyphilis?
back: A form of neurosyphilis presenting with personality and psychiatric changes.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is tabes dorsalis in neurosyphilis, and what tract is involved?
back: Involvement of the dorsal column tract, presenting with gait abnormalities.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the investigation of choice for neurosyphilis?
back: CSF-VDRL.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment for early syphilis (primary, secondary, and early latent)?
back: A single dose of injection benzathine penicillin, 2.4 million units, given deep IM.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment for late syphilis (late latent and tertiary, except neurosyphilis)?
back: Injection benzathine penicillin 2.4 million units deep IM, given as 3 doses, once weekly.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment for neurosyphilis?
back: Aqueous crystalline penicillin given IV for 10-14 days.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the pathophysiological sequence of the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction.
back: In a syphilitic patient on benzathine penicillin, lysis of T. pallidum releases lipoprotein into the blood, triggering release of IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-alpha, causing an acute inflammatory response.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the clinical features of the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction?
back: Fever, myalgia, hypotension, and worsening skin lesions within a few hours.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How is the Jarisch-Herxheimer reaction managed?
back: It is self-resolving; symptomatic treatment with NSAIDs can be given.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What organism causes chancroid?
back: Haemophilus ducreyi.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the soft chancre of chancroid.
back: Multiple, necrotic, non-indurated, tender lesions that bleed on touch.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the lymphadenopathy seen in chancroid.
back: A unilateral bubo — an enlarged, tender, pus-filled lymph node, often with a concomitant genital ulcer.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment for chancroid?
back: Tablet azithromycin 1 g single dose.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the characteristic microscopic finding in chancroid ulcer exudate?
back: Gram-negative coccobacilli arranged in long parallel strands, giving a 'school of fish/railroad track' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Why does herpes genitalis recur, and what is the underlying mechanism?
back: Due to viral latency in the sacral ganglia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which HSV type most commonly causes herpes genitalis, and what percentage does each type contribute?

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the inguinal lymphadenopathy in herpes genitalis.
back: Bilateral, enlarged, and tender.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the clinical progression of lesions in herpes genitalis.
back: Vesicles rupture to form multiple, grouped, tender ulcers whose margins fuse to create polycyclic margins.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What does Tzanck smear show in herpes genitalis?
back: Multinucleated giant cells.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the treatment for herpes genitalis?
back: Tablet acyclovir 400 mg TID for 7 days.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How is neonatal herpes prevented in a woman with active genital herpetic lesions at labor?
back: By performing a cesarean section.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What organism causes lymphogranuloma venereum (LGV)?
back: Chlamydia trachomatis serovars L1, L2, and L3.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the 1° stage of LGV.
back: A single, painless, transient ulcer.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the 2° stage of LGV, including the groove sign.
back: A bubo (two-thirds unilateral) without a concomitant genital ulcer; the groove sign of Greenblatt may be present or absent.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the 3° stage of LGV.
back: Genital elephantiasis due to destruction of lymphatics by chlamydia, causing permanent deformities.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What investigation and treatment are used for LGV?
back: NAAT for diagnosis; doxycycline 100 mg BD for 3 weeks for treatment.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What organism causes donovanosis (granuloma inguinale)?
back: Klebsiella granulomatis (also called Calymmatobacterium granulomatis).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the status of inguinal lymph nodes in donovanosis, and what is a 'pseudobubo'?
back: Inguinal lymph nodes are normal; a pseudobubo is a subcutaneous nodule in the inguinal region.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the ulcer seen in donovanosis.
back: A single, painless, beefy red ulcer that bleeds on touch, due to highly vascular granulation tissue.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What stain and technique are used to diagnose donovanosis, and what is seen?
back: A crushed tissue smear with Giemsa stain, showing mononuclear cells (Pund cells) with intracellular Donovan bodies (a 'closed safety pin' appearance).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the two treatment regimens for donovanosis, and for how long should treatment continue?
back: Azithromycin 1 g/week for 3 weeks, or azithromycin 500 mg/day for 3 weeks — given until the ulcer heals (minimum 3 weeks).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What causes of genital ulcer are single and non-tender?
back: Primary syphilis, LGV, and donovanosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What causes of genital ulcer are multiple and tender?
back: Chancroid and genital herpes.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the diagnosis when inguinal lymph nodes are not involved in genital ulcer disease?
back: Donovanosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the diagnosis when inguinal lymph nodes are bilateral and non-tender in genital ulcer disease?
back: Primary syphilis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the diagnosis when there is tender lymphadenopathy without a bubo (bubo negative)?
back: Genital herpes.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the diagnosis when there is a tender bubo with a concomitant genital ulcer?
back: Chancroid.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the diagnosis when there is a tender bubo without a concomitant genital ulcer?
back: LGV.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Summarize the pain characteristics of the genital ulcer and inguinal lymph node in syphilis.
back: Both the genital ulcer and inguinal lymph node are painless.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Summarize the pain characteristics of the genital ulcer and inguinal lymph node in chancroid.
back: Both are painful, with a positive (painful) bubo.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Summarize the pain characteristics of the genital ulcer and inguinal lymph node in genital herpes.
back: Both are painful, but the bubo is negative.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Summarize the pain characteristics of the genital ulcer and inguinal lymph node in LGV.
back: The ulcer is painless, but the inguinal lymph node/bubo is painful.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Summarize the pain characteristics of the genital ulcer and inguinal lymph node in donovanosis.
back: There is no pain in the ulcer, and no lymph node involvement.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How is a 'syndrome' defined in the context of STD management?
back: A group of clinical symptoms and signs.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: In what setting is syndromic management of STDs indicated?
back: Resource-poor settings (e.g., primary health centres) with no laboratory facilities.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the guiding principle of syndromic management?
back: To treat the most important organisms responsible for the syndrome.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 1 (grey) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for urethral discharge syndrome, vaginal discharge syndrome (cervicitis), painful scrotal swelling, and presumptive treatment of high-risk groups; contains tablet cefixime 800 mg single dose (for gonorrhea) and tablet azithromycin 1 g single dose (for chlamydia).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which high-risk groups receive presumptive treatment with Kit 1?
back: Female sex workers, men who have sex with men (MSM), and the bridge population.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 2 (green) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for vaginal discharge syndrome (vaginitis); contains tablet secnidazole 2 g (for bacterial vaginosis and trichomoniasis) and tablet fluconazole 150 mg single dose (for candidiasis).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 3 (white) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for genital ulcer disease syndrome (non-herpetic); contains injection benzathine penicillin 2.4 MU IM (for syphilis), tablet azithromycin 1 g single dose (for chancroid), plus a disposable syringe, needle, and sterile water.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 4 (blue) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for genital ulcer disease syndrome in penicillin allergy/unavailability; contains tablet doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 14 days (for syphilis) and tablet azithromycin 1 g single dose (for chancroid).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 5 (red) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for genital ulcer disease syndrome that is herpetic; contains tablet acyclovir 400 mg TID for 7 days (for genital herpes).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 6 (yellow) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for lower abdominal pain and pelvic inflammatory disease (polymicrobial); contains tablet cefixime 800 mg stat (gonorrhea), tablet doxycycline 100 mg BD for 14 days (chlamydia), and tablet metronidazole 400 mg BD for 14 days (anaerobic infections).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 7 (black) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for inguinal bubo (GUDS) and LGV proctitis (ano-rectal discharge syndrome); contains tablet doxycycline 100 mg BD for 3 weeks (for LGV).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Kit 8 (brown) used for, and what does it contain?
back: Used for ano-rectal discharge syndrome; contains tablet cefixime 800 mg stat (gonorrhea) and tablet doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7 days (chlamydia).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: In which three conditions is partner management not necessary?
back: Bacterial vaginosis, genital candidiasis (both due to alteration in normal vaginal flora), and genital herpes (due to asymptomatic viral shedding, since the virus remains latent in the body).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the underlying pathology of urticaria?
back: Dermal edema.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the lesion (wheal) of urticaria.
back: An itchy, transient plaque with central pallor and peripheral erythema.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is dermographism?
back: An exaggerated wheal-flare response elicited by stroking a blunt object on the skin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How is chronic spontaneous urticaria defined?
back: Urticaria lasting more than 6 weeks with no identifiable trigger.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the second-generation antihistamines used for chronic urticaria?
back: Cetirizine and levocetirizine.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What anti-IgE monoclonal antibody is used for chronic urticaria?
back: Omalizumab.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What calcineurin inhibitor is used for chronic urticaria?
back: Cyclosporine.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is angioedema, and what causes it?
back: Circumscribed, non-pitting edema of subcutaneous and submucosal tissues, due to increased vascular permeability.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the typical sites of angioedema?
back: The skin (lips, face, neck, extremities), and also the oral cavity, larynx, and gut.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the inheritance pattern of hereditary angioedema?
back: Autosomal dominant.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the clinical features of hereditary angioedema regarding wheals?
back: Recurrent episodes of non-pitting edema, with no wheals.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the underlying defect in hereditary angioedema?
back: Deficiency of C1 esterase inhibitor.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is cutaneous mastocytosis also called?
back: Urticaria pigmentosa.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the underlying pathology of cutaneous mastocytosis?
back: Mast cell proliferation in the skin, causing hyperpigmentation.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What stain is used to demonstrate mast cells in cutaneous mastocytosis, and what does it show?
back: Toluidine blue stain, showing metachromatic granules.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the Darier sign in cutaneous mastocytosis, and what causes it?
back: Erythema plus an urticarial halo produced on scratching lesional skin (dermographism does not occur on normal skin), due to mast cell degranulation and histamine release.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is eczema, in general terms?
back: A reaction pattern of the skin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the three types of eczema based on stage?
back: Acute, subacute, and chronic.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: List the features of acute eczema.
back: Erythema, edema, vesicles, oozing, and crusting.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: List the features of chronic eczema.
back: Hyperpigmentation, thick skin, and exaggerated skin marking, collectively called lichenification.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the two etiological classifications of eczema?
back: Exogenous and endogenous.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the two types of contact dermatitis (a form of exogenous eczema)?
back: Irritant contact dermatitis and allergic contact dermatitis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What proportion of contact dermatitis is irritant, and who is affected?
back: 80%; it occurs in all exposed people.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the pathophysiology and extent of irritant contact dermatitis?
back: Direct (non-immune) damage, restricted to the site of contact.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What proportion of contact dermatitis is allergic, and who is affected?
back: 20%; it occurs only in genetically predisposed individuals.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the pathophysiology and extent of allergic contact dermatitis?
back: A Type IV hypersensitivity reaction that tends to disseminate beyond the site of contact.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which metal commonly causes allergic contact dermatitis?
back: Nickel.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which compound in cement causes allergic contact dermatitis?
back: Potassium dichromate.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which compound in bindis causes allergic contact dermatitis?
back: Para-tertiary butyl phenol (PTBP).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which compound in hair dye causes allergic contact dermatitis?
back: Paraphenylene diamine (PPD).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Which topical antibiotic commonly causes allergic contact dermatitis?
back: Neomycin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What test is used to diagnose allergic contact dermatitis, and what type of hypersensitivity does it test for?
back: Patch test; it tests for Type 4 hypersensitivity.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How is the patch test performed and read?
back: Allergens are applied over the back, and the reading is taken after 48 hours (best read at 96 hours/4 days).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What plant causes airborne contact dermatitis, and how is it identified?
back: Parthenium hysterophorus, identified by its white flower-heads.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the allergen in Parthenium hysterophorus responsible for airborne contact dermatitis?
back: Sesquiterpene lactone (SQL).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What causes seborrheic dermatitis?
back: An inflammatory response to Malassezia furfur, a lipophilic fungus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the lesions of seborrheic dermatitis.
back: Greasy, yellow scales over the nasolabial folds.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is 'cradle cap' and in what condition is it seen?
back: Greasy, yellow scales over the scalp, seen in infantile seborrheic dermatitis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is stasis eczema (gravitational eczema) associated with?
back: Chronic venous insufficiency.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What CEAP classification stage corresponds to stasis eczema?
back: C4a (pigmentation/eczema).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the typical presentation of pityriasis alba.
back: A child with a history of atopy, presenting with hypopigmented, scaly macules on the face.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the differential diagnosis for pityriasis alba, and how is it distinguished?
back: Indeterminate leprosy, which lacks scaling (scaling is negative), unlike pityriasis alba.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe the appearance of nummular/discoid eczema.
back: A disc/coin-shaped lesion with active centre and periphery.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: Describe pompholyx (dyshidrotic eczema) and its typical sites.
back: Deep-seated vesicles with background eczema, affecting the palms and soles.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: How is atopic dermatitis defined?
back: A chronic, relapsing, pruritic inflammatory skin disease.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What type of hypersensitivity reaction is atopic dermatitis?
back: A localized, Type I hypersensitivity reaction.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What are the three components of the 'triad of atopy'?
back: Atopic asthma, atopic dermatitis, and allergic rhinitis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the timeline and distribution of the infantile phase of atopic dermatitis?
back: Up to 2 years of age; distribution involves the face, scalp, and extensors.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the timeline and distribution of the childhood phase of atopic dermatitis?
back: From 2 years to puberty; distribution involves flexures (cubital and popliteal fossa).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the timeline and distribution of the adult phase of atopic dermatitis?
back: After puberty; presents with lichenification of the flexures.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What criteria are used for diagnosing atopic dermatitis?
back: Hanifin and Rajka criteria.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the hallmark feature of atopic dermatitis according to Hanifin & Rajka criteria?
back: Pruritus, which is intermittent and intense.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: List the four Hanifin & Rajka diagnostic criteria for atopic dermatitis.
back: Pruritus (hallmark feature), typical morphology and distribution according to age, chronic/chronically relapsing dermatitis, and personal/family history of atopy.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the investigation of choice for atopic dermatitis?
back: Clinical examination.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is white dermographism in atopic dermatitis?
back: Abnormal vasoconstriction elicited on stroking the skin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the 'headlight sign' in atopic dermatitis?
back: Facial erythema with sparing of the perinasal and perioral areas.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the Dennie-Morgan fold in atopic dermatitis?
back: Infraorbital skin creases.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What eye changes can be seen in atopic dermatitis?
back: Keratoconus and shield cataract (anterior subscapular).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is Hertoghe's sign in atopic dermatitis?
back: Lateral madarosis (loss of the lateral eyebrows).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the topical treatment for atopic dermatitis?
back: Steroids and emollients.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the systemic treatment for atopic dermatitis?
back: Steroids or cyclosporine.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: List the clinical features of Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome.
back: Recurrent infections, atopic eczema, and thrombocytopenia (microthrombocytopenia).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-5: STIs, Syphilis, Allergic Disorders & Eczema
front: What is the inheritance pattern and gene defect in Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome?
back: X-linked recessive inheritance, caused by mutation in the WAS gene.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What type of hypersensitivity reaction is fixed drug eruption?
back: A Type 4 hypersensitivity reaction.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Name three drug classes that commonly cause fixed drug eruption.
back: NSAIDs, cotrimoxazole, and tetracyclines.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the lesion of fixed drug eruption.
back: A circular, dusky red plaque.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What happens on re-exposure to the offending drug in fixed drug eruption, and why?
back: The lesion recurs at the same (fixed) site, due to reactivation of memory T-cells.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: How does a fixed drug eruption lesion resolve?
back: It heals with hyperpigmentation.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What type of dermatitis is erythema multiforme, and what is its most common infectious trigger?
back: Cytotoxic dermatitis; the most common infectious trigger is HSV-1.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the three zones of the target lesion in erythema multiforme.
back: Centre: dusky; intermediate zone: edema; periphery: erythema.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the typical distribution of erythema multiforme lesions?
back: Distal extremities (palms and soles).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is Stevens-Johnson syndrome/toxic epidermal necrolysis (SJS-TEN)?
back: An acute, life-threatening, severe muco-cutaneous drug reaction.

subject: Dermatology
