deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What proportion of SJS-TEN cases are drug-induced?
back: More than 90%.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Which NSAID class is implicated in SJS-TEN?
back: Oxicams.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Which anti-epileptic drugs are implicated in SJS-TEN?
back: Phenytoin, carbamazepine, and lamotrigine.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Which other drug classes (besides NSAIDs and anti-epileptics) are implicated in SJS-TEN?
back: Sulfonamides, antiretrovirals (abacavir), and allopurinol.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the underlying pathogenesis of SJS-TEN?
back: Full-thickness epidermal keratinocyte necrosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: How is SJS-TEN classified based on body surface area (BSA) involvement?
back: Less than 10% BSA: SJS; 10-30% BSA: SJS-TEN overlap; more than 30% BSA: TEN.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Are systemic features present in SJS-TEN?
back: Yes, systemic features such as fever are present.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the skin lesions of SJS-TEN and how they differ from erythema multiforme's target lesions.
back: Flat, atypical target lesions with only 2 zones (unlike the 3 zones in erythema multiforme).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What additional skin finding is seen in SJS-TEN?
back: Sheet-like epidermal peeling.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the pseudo-Nikolsky sign in SJS-TEN, and what causes it?
back: A positive Nikolsky-like sign caused by keratinocyte necrosis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the typical distribution of skin lesions in SJS-TEN?
back: Proximal extremities and the trunk.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the mucosal findings in SJS-TEN.
back: Painful erosions with hemorrhagic crusts.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the first step in treating SJS-TEN?
back: Stop the offending drug.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What are additional treatment options for SJS-TEN?
back: Cyclosporine or IV immunoglobulins.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is another name for Neurofibromatosis-1, and what is its inheritance pattern?
back: Von Recklinghausen's disease; autosomal dominant.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What are cutaneous neurofibromas, and where do they arise from?
back: Tumors arising from the peripheral nerve sheath, typically seen in adolescence.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the appearance of cutaneous neurofibromas.
back: Skin-colored papulonodules.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the 'button hole sign' in cutaneous neurofibromas?
back: Invagination of the lesion into the skin when pressure is applied.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What are plexiform neurofibromas, and when do they present?
back: Tumors arising from multiple nerve fascicles; they are congenital.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the clinical characteristic of plexiform neurofibromas.
back: A diffuse, enlarging mass with a 'bag of worms' feel.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: How many café-au-lait macules are required for a diagnosis of NF-1?
back: 6 or more.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What size criteria for café-au-lait macules apply pre-pubertally and post-pubertally in NF-1?
back: Pre-pubertal: greater than 5 mm; post-pubertal: greater than 15 mm.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is Crowe's sign in NF-1?
back: Axillary freckling.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Besides axillary freckling, what other skin-fold freckling is seen in NF-1?
back: Inguinal freckling.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Is axillary freckling pathognomonic of NF-1?
back: Yes.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What are Lisch iris nodules in NF-1, and do they cause visual disturbance?
back: Pigmented iris hamartomas; they do not cause visual disturbance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is another name for Tuberous Sclerosis Complex, and what is its inheritance?
back: Bourneville's disease; autosomal dominant inheritance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What does the mnemonic EPILOIA (Vogt's triad) stand for in tuberous sclerosis?
back: Epilepsy, low intelligence, and adenoma sebaceum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the ash leaf macules of tuberous sclerosis.
back: Hypopigmented macules, typically found on the trunk.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is another name for adenoma sebaceum, and describe its appearance and site.
back: Facial angiofibromas; reddish-brown papules located in the malar area and nose.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What are periungual fibromas in tuberous sclerosis also called?
back: Koenen's tumors.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the shagreen patch of tuberous sclerosis, its cause, and location.
back: A leathery plaque due to increased collagen fibres, located in the lumbosacral region.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is another name for Sturge-Weber syndrome, and what is its inheritance pattern?
back: Encephalotrigeminal angiomatosis; sporadic inheritance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the CNS, eye, and skin features of Sturge-Weber syndrome.
back: CNS: epilepsy; eye: glaucoma; skin: a permanent port-wine stain (a capillary vascular malformation).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the treatment for the port-wine stain of Sturge-Weber syndrome?
back: Pulse dye laser.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: How is ichthyosis defined?
back: A disorder of keratinization producing fish-like scales, without inflammation.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the inheritance and defect in ichthyosis vulgaris, and describe the scale.
back: Autosomal dominant, with a filaggrin defect, producing fine, white scales.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the inheritance and defect in X-linked ichthyosis (ichthyosis nigra), and describe the scale.
back: X-linked recessive, with a steroid sulfatase defect, producing brown-black scales.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What associations are seen with X-linked ichthyosis?
back: Corneal opacities and cryptorchidism.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the inheritance and defect in lamellar ichthyosis?
back: Autosomal recessive, with an epidermal transglutaminase-1 defect.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the neonatal presentation of lamellar ichthyosis called?
back: A translucent, parchment-like membrane, called the collodion membrane.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the scales of lamellar ichthyosis and their distribution.
back: Dark, plate-like, adherent scales with no areas of sparing.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What associations are seen with lamellar ichthyosis?
back: Ectropion and eclabium.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Which condition results from an epidermal transglutaminase-3 defect?
back: Dermatitis herpetiformis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Name three disorders of DNA repair affecting the skin.
back: Xeroderma pigmentosum, Cockayne syndrome, and trichothiodystrophy.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the inheritance pattern of xeroderma pigmentosum?
back: Autosomal recessive.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the underlying defect in xeroderma pigmentosum?
back: A defect in nucleotide excision repair (NER).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the clinical features of xeroderma pigmentosum.
back: Photophobia, freckles (due to increased melanocyte activity), photosensitivity, xeroderma, and increased risk of skin malignancies (SCC, BCC).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What do the words 'tricho' and 'thio' refer to in trichothiodystrophy?
back: 'Tricho' refers to hair and 'thio' refers to sulphur.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the inheritance pattern of trichothiodystrophy?
back: Autosomal recessive.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the pathogenic sequence in trichothiodystrophy.
back: Abnormal synthesis of sulphur-containing keratins leads to defective disulfide linkages, resulting in brittle, fragile hair.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the clinical hair findings in trichothiodystrophy.
back: Diffuse, sparse, and broken hair.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the 'tiger tail appearance' seen under polarizing microscopy in trichothiodystrophy?
back: Alternating light and dark bands along the hair shaft.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What deficiency is phrynoderma associated with?
back: Essential fatty acid deficiency and/or vitamin A deficiency.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What vitamin deficiency causes scurvy?
back: Vitamin C deficiency.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the clinical features of scurvy.
back: Corkscrew hair, perifollicular hemorrhage, and bleeding gums.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Which organism is associated with 'corkscrew motility' as a differential term to remember alongside scurvy's corkscrew hair?
back: Treponema pallidum.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What vitamin deficiency causes pellagra?
back: Vitamin B3 (niacin) deficiency.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What dietary and lifestyle factors are associated with pellagra?
back: Maize and jowar-rich diets, and chronic alcoholism.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the clinical triad of pellagra?
back: Dermatitis, dermatitis, and diarrhea — specifically, dementia, dermatitis, and diarrhea.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What deficiency causes acrodermatitis enteropathica, and what is its inheritance?
back: Zinc deficiency; autosomal recessive inheritance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the molecular defect in acrodermatitis enteropathica?
back: A defect in the zinc transporter protein ZIP4, encoded by the SLC39A4 gene, causing decreased zinc absorption.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the clinical triad of acrodermatitis enteropathica?
back: Dermatitis (periorificial around eyes, nose, mouth, genitals, and acral dermatitis), diarrhea, and alopecia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the treatment for acrodermatitis enteropathica?
back: Lifelong zinc supplementation.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What skin finding is seen with vitamin B12 deficiency?
back: Knuckle hyperpigmentation.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What anemia-related findings are seen in vitamin B12 deficiency?
back: Pallor and glossitis (beefy red tongue).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What CNS findings are seen in vitamin B12 deficiency?
back: Numbness, tingling, and memory loss.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is an important differential diagnosis for knuckle hyperpigmentation, and what other features characterize it?
back: Addison's disease, characterized by fatigue, weight loss, decreased blood pressure, and electrolyte imbalance (decreased sodium, increased potassium).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: Describe the malar (butterfly) rash of acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus.
back: Erythema over the malar area and the bridge of the nose.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: Is photosensitivity present in acute cutaneous lupus erythematosus, and is the nasolabial fold involved?
back: Photosensitivity is present, but the nasolabial fold is spared.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What is the most common lesion of chronic cutaneous lupus erythematosus (CCLE)?
back: Discoid lupus erythematosus (discoid LE).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What is the typical distribution of discoid LE lesions?
back: Face, scalp, and ears.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: Describe the centre and periphery of a discoid LE plaque.
back: Centre shows atrophy and scarring; periphery shows hyperpigmentation with a positive carpet tack sign.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What additional features are seen with discoid LE plaques?
back: Adherent scales and scarring alopecia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What type of disease is dermatomyositis, and what two systems does it affect?
back: An autoimmune disease affecting the skin and skeletal muscle (proximal myopathy).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What are Gottron's papules in dermatomyositis?
back: Violaceous, flat-topped papules over the MCP/IP joints.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What is the heliotrope rash in dermatomyositis?
back: Violaceous, macular periorbital erythema, more prominent over the upper eyelid.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What is 'mechanic's hand' in dermatomyositis?
back: Hyperkeratotic fissures over the fingertips and sides of the fingers.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Lupus & Dermatomyositis
front: What is the shawl sign in dermatomyositis?
back: Violaceous, macular erythema over the shoulders and upper back.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: What does the term 'sclero' mean in scleroderma?
back: Thick.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: What is morphea, and what areas does it involve?
back: Localized scleroderma, involving skin changes only.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: Describe the lesion of morphea.
back: A violaceous, indurated plaque.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: What is the histopathological (HPE) finding in morphea, and what causes it?
back: A 'box-like' appearance of the dermis on biopsy, due to increased collagen.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: What is 'en coup de sabre', and what is it a variant of?
back: A variant of morphea presenting with linear scarring alopecia of the forehead and scalp.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: What are the two types of systemic sclerosis based on extent of skin involvement?
back: Limited cutaneous systemic sclerosis (distal to elbow/knee plus face) and diffuse cutaneous systemic sclerosis (distal plus proximal to elbow/knee plus trunk involvement).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: What does the CREST mnemonic stand for in limited systemic sclerosis (CREST syndrome)?
back: Calcinosis cutis, Raynaud phenomenon, Esophageal dysmotility, Sclerodactyly, and Telangiectasia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: Describe the facial features of systemic sclerosis.
back: Mask-like facies, pinched nose, perioral furrows, restricted mouth opening (microstomia), and mat-like telangiectasia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Scleroderma
front: What is the characteristic pigmentation change seen in systemic sclerosis?
back: 'Salt and pepper' pigmentation.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Vasculitis & Reactive Arthritis
front: What type of vasculitis is Henoch-Schönlein purpura, and what antibody class is involved?
back: A small vessel vasculitis; it is an IgA vasculitis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Vasculitis & Reactive Arthritis
front: Describe the skin lesions of Henoch-Schönlein purpura.
back: Symmetrical, palpable purpura over the lower limbs.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Vasculitis & Reactive Arthritis
front: What is the platelet count in Henoch-Schönlein purpura?
back: Normal.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Vasculitis & Reactive Arthritis
front: How is reactive arthritis defined?
back: Inflammatory arthritis occurring secondary to a distant focus of infection (e.g., urethritis).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Vasculitis & Reactive Arthritis
front: What is circinate balanitis, seen in reactive arthritis?
back: Circular, painless erosions on the glans penis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
topic: Skin in Connective Tissue Diseases - Vasculitis & Reactive Arthritis
front: What is keratoderma blennorrhagicum, seen in reactive arthritis?
back: Hyperkeratotic papules and plaques on the palms and soles.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What type of panniculitis is erythema nodosum?
back: Septal panniculitis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the clinical lesions of erythema nodosum, and how do they heal?
back: Red, tender nodules on the anterior leg, which heal without scarring.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the most common cause of erythema nodosum, and what is the most common infectious trigger?
back: Idiopathic is the most common cause overall; Group A Streptococci is the most common infectious trigger.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is Löfgren's syndrome?
back: Fever, bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy, arthritis, and erythema nodosum occurring together.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the most common cause of acanthosis nigricans, and what is a rarer cause?
back: Obesity (most common cause); GI malignancy (rare cause).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the skin lesions of acanthosis nigricans and their location.
back: Hyperpigmented, velvety plaques at the axilla and neck folds.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What type of disorder is granuloma annulare, and what systemic disease is it associated with?
back: A necrobiotic disorder (collagen degeneration), associated with diabetes mellitus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the lesions of granuloma annulare.
back: An annular arrangement of papules on the dorsum of the extremities.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What systemic disease is necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum associated with?
back: Diabetes mellitus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the lesion of necrobiosis lipoidica diabeticorum and its location.
back: A waxy, yellow, atrophic plaque with surface telangiectasia, on the anterior aspect of the leg.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is another name for pretibial myxedema, and what condition is it associated with?
back: Thyroid dermopathy; associated with Graves' disease.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the lesion of pretibial myxedema and its location.
back: Diffuse, non-pitting edema on the anterior aspect of the leg.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What condition is xanthelasma palpebrarum associated with, and how frequently?
back: Hyperlipidemia, in about 50% of cases.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the lesion of xanthelasma palpebrarum and its location.
back: Symmetric, firm, yellow papules at the eyelid margin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is mycosis fungoides, and what type of lymphoma does it represent?
back: A cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL), a type of non-Hodgkin's lymphoma involving CD4 T-cells.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is a Pautrier's microabscess, seen in the histopathology of mycosis fungoides?
back: A collection of atypical T-cells within the epidermis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is Sezary syndrome?
back: The leukemic variant of cutaneous T-cell lymphoma (CTCL), presenting with erythroderma involving more than 90% of body surface area.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the clinical triad of Sezary syndrome?
back: Erythroderma, generalised lymphadenopathy, and circulating Sezary cells.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the characteristic nuclear feature of Sezary cells?
back: A cerebriform nucleus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is another name for basal cell carcinoma, and describe its invasiveness and metastatic potential.
back: Rodent ulcer; it is locally invasive, but metastasis is rare.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the initial morphology of a basal cell carcinoma.
back: A translucent, pearly nodule with surface telangiectasia.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the progression of basal cell carcinoma morphology after central necrosis.
back: It progresses to a rolled-out ulcer with beaded margins.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What type of disease is lichen nitidus, and is Koebner's phenomenon positive?
back: A chronic inflammatory disease of unknown etiology; Koebner's phenomenon is positive, with new lesions forming along lines of trauma.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the clinical lesions and sites of lichen nitidus.
back: Asymptomatic, pinhead-sized, shiny papules, found on the forearm, elbow, and penile shaft.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the 'claw clutching the ball' histopathological appearance in lichen nitidus?
back: The 'ball' represents chronic inflammatory cell infiltrate, and the 'claw' represents the surrounding rete ridges.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is cutis marmorata, and in whom is it seen?
back: A marbled appearance of the skin due to a physiological response to cold, seen in infants.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Where does cutis marmorata occur, and does it reverse with warming?
back: It occurs on the trunk and extremities, and reverses on warming.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What type of disorder is dermatitis artefacta?
back: A psychocutaneous disorder that is self-induced.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the lesion and typical site of dermatitis artefacta.
back: Superficial, bizarre erosions, occurring at sites accessible to the patient's hands.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What condition is characterized by a bizarre pattern of hair loss due to self-induced pulling?
back: Trichotillomania.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is methylisothiazolinone (MIT) used for, and what is its action?
back: A preservative used in cosmetics and personal care products; it inhibits bacterial growth.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is NAME syndrome, and what is its inheritance?
back: An autosomal dominant subtype of Carney complex.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the clinical features of NAME syndrome.
back: Nevi, atrial myxoma, myxoid neurofibroma, and ephelides/freckles.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the skin lesion pattern of flagellate dermatitis.
back: Linear/curvilinear streaks.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the most common trigger of flagellate dermatitis?
back: Bleomycin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the mechanism leading to cutaneous toxicity in bleomycin-induced flagellate dermatitis.
back: A lack of bleomycin hydrolase in the skin causes accumulation of bleomycin after its use, leading to cutaneous toxicity.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What antiretroviral drug causes hyperpigmentation of the palms/soles?
back: Emtricitabine.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What antiretroviral drug causes hyperpigmentation of the palms/soles/nails?
back: Zidovudine.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the eczematous dermatoses that are indications for topical corticosteroids.
back: Atopic dermatitis and seborrheic dermatitis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the papulosquamous diseases that are indications for topical corticosteroids.
back: Psoriasis and lichen planus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the autoimmune diseases that are indications for topical corticosteroids.
back: Vitiligo and connective tissue disease (CTD).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What does the STEROID mnemonic stand for regarding side effects of topical corticosteroids?
back: S: skin thinning (atrophy, striae); T: telangiectasia; E: ecchymosis/easy bruisability; R: rosacea-like dermatitis; O: opportunistic infections (e.g., tinea incognito); I: induced acne, increased hair growth (hypertrichosis); D: decreased pigmentation, delayed healing.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Why are topical steroids not used to treat rosacea?
back: Because they can cause a rosacea-like dermatitis.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is cutaneous malakoplakia, and in whom does it occur?
back: A chronic granulomatous response to infective agents, occurring in immunocompromised individuals.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the risk factors for cutaneous malakoplakia.
back: HIV infection, renal transplantation, neoplasms, and immunosuppressive therapy.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the most common organisms causing cutaneous malakoplakia.
back: E. coli, Klebsiella, and Proteus.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the underlying immune defect in cutaneous malakoplakia?
back: An acquired bactericidal defect of macrophages.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the lesion and most common site of cutaneous malakoplakia.
back: A yellowish-red plaque, most commonly in the perianal region.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is seen on histopathology of cutaneous malakoplakia, and what are these cells called?
back: Sheets of large macrophages, called Von Hansemann cells.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the hallmark histopathological finding of cutaneous malakoplakia, and describe its appearance.
back: Michaelis-Gutmann bodies — intracytoplasmic, basophilic, concentric bodies with a 'target' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What type of disorder is lichen sclerosus, and what areas does it affect?
back: A chronic inflammatory mucocutaneous disorder affecting genital and extragenital skin.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: Describe the progression of lichen sclerosus lesions.
back: Porcelain-white papules and plaques progress to sclerotic and atrophic lesions, giving a 'cellophane paper-type' appearance.

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: List the complications of lichen sclerosus.
back: Atrophy, destructive scarring, functional impairment, and malignancy (SCC).

subject: Dermatology
deck: Part-6: Drug Reactions, Genodermatoses, Systemic Associations & Miscellaneous
front: What is the treatment for lichen sclerosus?
back: Topical corticosteroid.
